0: <title>Carbon footprint of milk production under smallholder dairying in Anand district of Western India: a cradle-to-farm gate life cycle assessment </title>
M. R. GargA,C, B. T. Phondba^, P. L. SherasiaA and H. P. S. Makkar

Animal Nutrition Group, National Dairy Development Board, Anand-388 001, Gujarat, India. Animal Production and Health Division, Food and Agriculture Organisation of the United Nations (FAO), Viale delle Terme di Caracalla, 00153 Rome, Italy. Corresponding author. Email: mrgarg@nddb.coop

Abstract. In recent years, the concept of life cycle assessment (LCA) has proven to be useful because of its potential to assess the integral environmental impacts of agricultural products. Developing countries such as India are good candidates for LCA research because of the large contribution of smallholder dairy system to the production of agricultural products such as milk. Therefore, the aim of the present study was to explore the carbon footprint of milk production under the multi-functional smallholder dairy system in Anand district of Gujarat state, western India. A cradle-to-farm gate LCA was performed by covering 60 smallholder dairy farms within 12 geographically distinct villages of the district. The average farm size was 4.0 animals per farm, and the average number of each category of animal was 2.5 lactating cows, 1.4 lactating buffaloes, 1.8 replacement cows, 1.6 replacement buffaloes, 2.0 retired cows, 1.3 retired buffaloes and 1.0 ox per farm. The emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) on CO2-equivalent (CO-eq) basis from feed production, enteric fermentation and manure management were allocated to fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) on the basis of mass balance, price and digestibility. Emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O from cattle contributed 11.0%, 75.4% and 13.6%, respectively, to the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The contribution of CO2, CH4 and N20 from buffalo was 8.2%, 80.5% and 11.3%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions of farms. The average carbon footprint (CF) of cow milk was 2.3, 1.9 and 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM on mass, economic and digestibility basis, respectively, whereas for buffalo, milk CF was 3.0, 2.5 and 2.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, respectively. On the basis of digestibility allocation, emissions from retired (>10 years of age and incapable of or ceased producing milk) COWS and buffaloes were 1571.3 and 2556.1 kg CO2-eq/retirement year, respectively. Overall, the CF of milk production under the smallholder dairy system in Anand district was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, which reduced to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM when milk, manure, finance and insurance were considered as economic functions of the smallholder system. The CF was lower by 65% and 22% for cow and buffalo milk, respectively, than were the estimates of FAO for southern Asia, and this was mainly attributed to difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure management systems, feed digestibility and milk production data used by FAO.
Additional keywords: attributional life cycle assessment, climate change, co-product allocation, emission intensity.
Received 18 August 2015, accepted 6 November 2015, published online 9 February 2016

3: Climate change is transforming the planet's ecosystem and threatening the well-being of current and future generations. Livestock are believed to be a significant contributor of climate change, representing 14.5% of global anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. Worldwide, dairy and beef production account for 20% and 41%, respectively, of the emissions of this sectors followed by buffalo milk and meat, with 8% of the emissions (Gerber et al. 2013). Rapid population growth and shifting trends in dietary patterns towards more dairy and meat products will further result in increased emissions unless production efficiencies and management are significantly improved.
Mixed crop-livestock systems produce the bulk of livestock products in the developing world, i.e. 75% of the milk and 60% of the meat, and also employ many millions of people in

farms, formal and informal markets, processing plants and other parts of supply chains (Herrero et al. 2010). These systems convert waste material and other resources of limited alternative use into human-edible products and other goods and services. Livestock farms in developing countries are predominately managed by smallholders, with 80% of land holdings being smaller than 10 ha (FAO 2012). In smallholder dairy systems that integrate crop growing with livestock raising, livestock are kept not only to produce milk or meat, but also to produce manure for fertilising croplands and to pull ploughs, and are also considered as major capital assets. Thus, in contrast to industrialised countries, where livestock are produced in 'industrial' production systems for single products (such as e.g. milk, beef), farm animals in such smallholder settings are predominantly 'multi-functional' (Weiler et al. 2014).

As a result of continuous pressure on the livestock sector to reduce its impact on climate change, many researchers worldwide are intensely studying livestock emissions and finding ways to reduce them. However, the multi-functionality of small-scale livestock systems in developing countries presents a major challenge when it comes to assessing the levels of GHGs emitted by a smallholder system. The situation is further exacerbated by the heterogeneity of smallholder systems and landscapes and the diversity of methods used. Global study of dairy production systems conducted using life cycle assessment (LCA) approach indicated that the extensive systems with rather low milk yields have higher GHG emissions per unit of product and, therefore, higher carbon footprints (CF) than do more intensive systems with higher milk yields (FAO 2010). Several researchers have investigated CF of dairy or beef under European and North American specialised production systems (Casey and Holden 2005; Thomassen et al. 2008a; Capper et al. 2009). Contrary to specialised systems, outputs of mixed systems vary with different climatic and cultural settings all around the world. For instance, Ethiopian and Kenyan smallholder dairy farms produced multiple outputs that include milk, meat and manure, cattle as insurance, finance and social status (Modupeore 2011; Woldegebriel 2013). Traditionally, Indian smallholders keep dairy animals for milk, manure, draught power and for cultural values (Kurup 2002; Thirunavukkarasu et al. 2014). Therefore, a comparison solely on the basis of quantity of milk or meat produced does not reflect the complexity of the mixed production systems operations.

Our understanding of the contribution of smallholder farming to climate change is remarkably limited. There has been very limited research on LCA considering multiple functions of livestock in developing countries and none of them has been conducted in India. Concerns over poor state of information on environmental footprint of smallholder dairy systems have driven us to quantify CF of milk production considering the multi- functionality of Indian smallholder farms.

7: System description The study involved 60 smallholder farms from 12 geographically distinct villages and these farms were random samples of the mixed crop-livestock system in Anand district of Gujarat state. Data were collected between July 2014 and June 2015. Anand district is situated in 'Western plane and hill' (agro-ecological subregion of India) with an average altitude of 43 m above mean sea level and average rainfall of 687 mm/year (GOI 2015). Many smallholder farms in this region grow cash crops, mostly tobacco and banana; and rear limited dairy animals for household milk consumption. The surplus milk produced at farm was sold to the village-level dairy cooperative societies and, thereafter, it was carried to the district-level processing plant.
8: A stepwise approach was used for the data collection from field. The field survey was divided into open-ended and semi-structured interviews. At first, an open-ended interview was conducted by visiting 10 smallholder households in Anand taluka, to get an

understanding of the local situations of dairying, understanding bovine functions and their meaning to the farmers. The choice of method for open-ended interview was qualitative ethnographic technique (Schlehe 2008; Weiler et al. 2014). It also enabled us to set the format of questionnaire for the semi-structured interviews. On the basis of the questionnaire prepared through open-ended interviews as a first step, semi-structured interviews were conducted with a separate group of 60 farmers from six taluka places within the district (Fig. 1).

Depending on the geographical distribution, two villages situated 10-15 km apart from each other in opposite directions were selected from each taluka. Within each village, five randomly distributed smallholder households were interviewed. During semi-structured interviews, detailed information about the household, cultivation of crops, and feeding and management of livestock was collected. Two taluka places located in north-west of the district were not considered in the present assessment due to sparse distribution of livestock farms in this area.
9: A stepwise approach was used for the data collection from field. The field survey was divided into open-ended and semi-structured interviews. At first, an open-ended interview was conducted by visiting 10 smallholder households in Anand taluka, to get an

understanding of the local situations of dairying, understanding bovine functions and their meaning to the farmers. The choice of method for open-ended interview was qualitative ethnographic technique (Schlehe 2008; Weiler et al. 2014). It also enabled us to set the format of questionnaire for the semi-structured interviews. On the basis of the questionnaire prepared through open-ended interviews as a first step, semi-structured interviews were conducted with a separate group of 60 farmers from six taluka places within the district (Fig. 1).

Depending on the geographical distribution, two villages situated 10-15 km apart from each other in opposite directions were selected from each taluka. Within each village, five randomly distributed smallholder households were interviewed. During semi-structured interviews, detailed information about the household, cultivation of crops, and feeding and management of livestock was collected. Two taluka places located in north-west of the district were not considered in the present assessment due to sparse distribution of livestock farms in this area.
10: The CF of a product is the sum of the net GHG emitted throughout the life cycle of the product within a set of system boundaries and in relation to a defined amount of a specified product (IDF 2010). To calculate the CF of agricultural products, an LCA is needed where all inputs and outputs of the system are considered up to the farm-gate, or beyond, in the production cycle (Flysjö et al. 2011). For computing CF of milk, we followed the ISO standardised methods (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) and the IDF (2010) guide on standard LCA methodology for the dairy sector. The GHG emissions were estimated using the Tier 2 approach of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC 2006). The four steps of LCA are briefly described below.
11: The goal of the present LCA study was to calculate the CF of milk produced at smallholder dairy systems in Anand. Economic functions of the smallholder systems included in the present assessment were production of milk, manure and role of bovines as finance and insurance. The functional unit was '1 kg of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) produced at the farm-gate', expressed as kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-eq) per kilogram of FPCM. Total cow milk produced at the farm was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein, using the following FAO (2010) equation:
Buffalo milk production was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.1% protein using the following Di Palo (1992) equation:
FPCM (kg) = milk production (1 + 0.011 ((10 X fat (%) 40) + (10 X protein (%) 31))).
The scope of the LCA indicates the overall approach used to establish the system boundary, which determines which unit processes are included in the LCA and reflects the goal of the

Fig. 1. Location of selected villages within Anand district.

study (IDF 2010). In the present assessment, on-farm and off- farm processes related to the dairy production were analysed. On- farm processes were mostly the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy cattle, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuels. Off-farm processes included those related to the off-farm dairy activities, i.e. inputs involved in production, processing and transportation of external feeds. A 'cradle-to- farm gate' system boundary included all upstream processes in livestock production up to the point where the co-products (milk and manure) left the farm (Fig. 2). An attributional approach of LCA was applied in the present assessment to imply emissions under the current production scenario (Thomassen et al. 2008b). Allocation problem usually occurs when concentrate ingredients are part of multi-functional process and when the environmental burden is divided between milk and other products of the system. LCA has a product focus and the guidelines of LCA (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) provide rules on how to allocate the environmental impact of a process in the case of multiple outputs. In the present assessment, multiple functions of livestock (i.e. milk, manure, finance and insurance) were handled as multiple products of the production system. As the other functions of the system such as draught power and role of cattle as cultural value were difficult to quantify, they were not considered while allocating environmental burden. The complexity involved in these functions is discussed separately under the discussion section of the present paper. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has honoured and worshiped in Indian culture. In Gujarat, slaughter of cattle (cow, calf, bull and bullock) is prohibited by law under the Gujarat Animal Preservation (Amendment) Act 2011. Slaughter of unfit animals such as she-buffalo (incapable of or ceased producing milk), buffalo bull and bullock (incapable

of breeding or working as draught animal) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old unproductive COWS and buffaloes were retained by the farmers. Surplus male calves from cows and buffaloes were neither reared nor sent for slaughter by the farmers. Such calves were let loose by the farmers in nearby villages. Therefore, meat was not considered as a co-product in the present assessment. The allocation procedure described by ISO 14044 (2006) states that, whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. When allocation cannot be avoided, the inputs and outputs of the system should be partitioned between its different products in a way that it reflects the underlying physical relationships among them, i.e. mass balance. Where physical relationship cannot be established, the inputs and outputs should be allocated among the products in a way that reflects other relationships among them; for example, inputs and outputs might be allocated among co- products in proportion to the economic value of the products. Allocation among co-products can also be undertaken using the biological method as recommended by IDF (2010), on the basis of the energy required to produce the amount of each co-product leaving the farm. In compliance with these rules, the mass, economic and digestibility allocations were applied only to those inputs in which human inedible crop by-products and residues were used for feeding of animals. Mass allocation was applied by computing the proportion of each feed by- product or residue in total quantity of biomass produced after considering harvesting losses. Economic allocation was applied on the basis of proportion of crop by-products or residues in total economic value of respective crop. For computing biological allocation, proportion of digestible DM of each feed ingredient was considered. The digestibility (%) of various feed ingredients was obtained from Feedipedia (2015). GHG emissions were
Fig. 2. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary (-) indicating process (-) and emission flows.

allocated between co-products (milk and manure) and other functions (finance and insurance) on the basis of their economic value. The details of allocation factors used in the present assessment are presented in Table 1.

Life cycle inventory encompasses all primary and secondary technical data related to the processes within the system boundary, e.g. details of animal husbandry, manure management, feed production, feed basket and products produced in dairy system. The primary data were collected directly from the farmers, whereas secondary data were obtained from peer-reviewed literature. The details of input data, along with their sources, are summarised in this section.

In the LCA model, cows and buffaloes were divided into categories like lactating animals, replacement animals (heifers and pregnant females), retired animals (old unproductive) and oxen. Cows and buffaloes above 10 years of age and incapable of or ceased producing milk were considered as retired. The herd composition of each smallholder farm was obtained at the time of field visits. Heart girth of animals was measured during field visits and bodyweights were calculated as per Shaeffer's formula (Sastry et al. 1982; Garg et al. 2013), as follows:
Bodyweight (kg) = ((heart girth (cm)/2.54) X length of 2 the body (cm)/2.54)/300) X 0.4536).
Farmers were asked about annual average daily milk production of animals, and the quantities of milk consumed at home and sold
for processing were recorded. The average fat content and the price of milk were recorded from the receipts given by the dairy cooperative societies to the farmers after sale of milk.

For on-farm feed products, the details of crop-wise cultivation practices such as area under cultivation, application rate of synthetic fertilisers, manure, pesticides and seeds, yields of human edible crop products, inedible crop by-products and residues, use of draught power, tractor and fossil fuels were recorded through interviews. Details of off-farm feed products such as purchased cattle feed, by-products of concentrates and oilseed meals, along with their source, mode of transport and annual average price, were obtained from farmers. All off-farm feed products were purchased from various states of India. The composition of cattle feed was obtained from a local feed factory situated in Anand. To estimate the quantity of feed inputs at farm, feeding calendars were prepared after discussion with the farmers. These estimates were then translated into kg DM for each feed by applying literature-based DM conversion factors (Feedipedia 2015).

The quantities of manure produced, used as fertiliser for on- farm crops, sold as fertiliser and used as fuel in the form of dung cakes were obtained on the basis of information provided by farmers. Economic value of manure as a fertiliser was obtained from local market in each village (Scoones 1992). The nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium content of solid manure were considered as 0.5%, 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively (Pennington et al. 2009).
In smallholder system, animals could serve as means of finance to the farmers. Therefore, the role of bovines as finance was considered as the avoidance of paying an interest
Table 1. Allocation factors (%) used for feed inputs and co-products produced at farms

Parameter|Mass allocation|Economic allocation|Digestibility allocation Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs Pearl millet grain|20.4|31.1|30.9 Maize grain|39.5|47.4|51.7 Maize gluten meal|2.2|3.2|3.0 Maize bran|7.3|12.1|7.8 Rice polish (16% oil)|1.7|1.6|2.3 Rice bran|4.2|4.8|5.2 De-oiled rice bran|3.6|1.7|4.2 Wheat bran|5.5|3.9|6.2 Soybean chunni|42.2|49.1|51.2 Pigeon pea chunni|3.1|3.7|3.7 Cottonseed meal|4.4|7.9|7.4 Guar meal|16.2|5.6|20.5 Maize germ meal|1.4|1.3|1.5 Rapeseed meal|34.7|28.7|43.5 Sorghum straw|65.0|36.7|52.9 Millet straw|71.4|52.0|57.6 Paddy straw|57.2|23.8|37.2 Wheat straw|49.7|20.2|35.9 Molasses|2.5|3.3|5.4 Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions Milk||78.8| Manure||4.2| Finance||12.5| Insurance||4.5|

by the farmers when borrowing money from a bank or an informal money lender (Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as finance was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and an interest rate on finance. Economic value of cattle and buffalo was obtained after consultation with insurance companies in the district. For all farms, an interest rate of 14% was applied, as it was the minimum interest rate on personal finance offered by the nationalised bank in India.

The insurance function of livestock was the opportunity to sell animals when needed and, thus, having animals was equal to having insurance (Moll 2005). The benefit of bovines as insurance was understood as the absence to pay a premium in case of insurance (Bebe et al. 2002; Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as an insurance for the household was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and the insurance premium, i.e. the cost that farmers would need to pay to purchase insurance coverage equal to the capital value of their herd. An insurance premium of 5% was applied for all farms after consultation with the national insurance company in India.

The most important GHGs related to smallholder dairy systems were CO2, methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions of CO2 resulted from the combustion of fossil fuel to power farm machinery and from electricity used in feed production and processing. The CO2 emissions from diesel were computed using the default emission factor (EF), as per IPCC (2006) guidelines. Soil carbon fluxes for on-farm and off- farm crops were excluded from the present assessment.

Methane was produced by ruminants mainly as a by-product of enteric fermentation. For estimating enteric CH4 emissions, average daily feed intake expressed as gross energy intake was calculated from the diet for cattle and buffalo. Enteric CH4 emissions were estimated as a function of intake, using the conversion factor (Ym = 6.5% of gross energy intake) and computing EF for each animal category (IPCC 2006, table 10.12, eqn 10.21). Methane emissions from manure were estimated as a proportion of the maximum CH4 potential (Bo) of manure volatile solids (VS) excreted (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.23). Estimated excretions of manure VS were obtained using the DM intake and diet digestibility (IPCC 2006; eqn 10.24). The proportion of Bo that was emitted from manure VS was computed using specific CH4 conversion factor (MCF) for solid manure storage system, i.e. 5.0% (IPCC 2006, table 10.17). The MCF was selected considering an annual average ambient temperature >28°C for Anand district.

Emissions of N2O from manure were derived after calculating nitrogen (N) excretion, which was estimated as the total N in feed intake minus the N in milk and that was retained in animals (calf and heifer growth). Direct N2O emissions from the stored manure were estimated using the corresponding default EF for solid manure storage system, i.e. 0.005 kg N2O-N/kg N excreted (IPCC 2006, table 10.21, eqn 10.25). For synthetic N fertiliser, the default EF was 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N applied. Volatilisation of NH3 and leaching of NO3 contributed to indirect emissions of N20. Losses of N through volatilisation (NH3 and NOX) and leaching from solid manure storage system were considered as 30% and 4%, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 10.22). The fraction of N that volatilised as NH3 and NO under different conditions was considered as 0.10 and 0.20 kg/kg of N from synthetic or organic fertilisers applied, respectively, and N loss by leaching was considered as 0.30 kg/kg of N applied to soil (IPCC 2006, table 11.3). Default EF for N2O emissions was 0.01 and 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg of N volatilised and leached, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 11.3).

The focus of the present LCA study was on impact category climate change. Total GHG emissions included CO2, CH4 and N2O from feed production (cultivation, processing and transport), CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N20 from manure management. The sum of GHG emissions was expressed in CO2-equivalents after considering the global warming potential of CO2 as 1, CH4 as 25 and N20 as 298 (IPCC 2007), which have been revised for CH4 as 28 and N2O as 265 (IPCC 2013).
12: The goal of the present LCA study was to calculate the CF of milk produced at smallholder dairy systems in Anand. Economic functions of the smallholder systems included in the present assessment were production of milk, manure and role of bovines as finance and insurance. The functional unit was '1 kg of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) produced at the farm-gate', expressed as kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-eq) per kilogram of FPCM. Total cow milk produced at the farm was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein, using the following FAO (2010) equation:
Buffalo milk production was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.1% protein using the following Di Palo (1992) equation:
FPCM (kg) = milk production (1 + 0.011 ((10 X fat (%) 40) + (10 X protein (%) 31))).
The scope of the LCA indicates the overall approach used to establish the system boundary, which determines which unit processes are included in the LCA and reflects the goal of the

"Gujarat Anand Umreth N Khambhat Tarapur [ ] Sojitra Petlad [X] Borsad [X] [X] Anand [ ] Ankla [ ]"| "Gujarat Anand Umreth N Khambhat Tarapur [ ] Sojitra Petlad [X] Borsad [X] [X] Anand [ ] Ankla [ ]"|Village [ ] Ashi [ ] Asodar [X] Dahewan [ ] Davol [ ] Gopalpura [ ] Lunej [X] Navakhal [X] Sandesar [X] Shekhadi [ ] Sundalpura [ ] Thamna [X] Vatadara [ ] Taluka
Fig. 1. Location of selected villages within Anand district.

study (IDF 2010). In the present assessment, on-farm and off- farm processes related to the dairy production were analysed. On- farm processes were mostly the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy cattle, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuels. Off-farm processes included those related to the off-farm dairy activities, i.e. inputs involved in production, processing and transportation of external feeds. A 'cradle-to- farm gate' system boundary included all upstream processes in livestock production up to the point where the co-products (milk and manure) left the farm (Fig. 2). An attributional approach of LCA was applied in the present assessment to imply emissions under the current production scenario (Thomassen et al. 2008b). Allocation problem usually occurs when concentrate ingredients are part of multi-functional process and when the environmental burden is divided between milk and other products of the system. LCA has a product focus and the guidelines of LCA (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) provide rules on how to allocate the environmental impact of a process in the case of multiple outputs. In the present assessment, multiple functions of livestock (i.e. milk, manure, finance and insurance) were handled as multiple products of the production system. As the other functions of the system such as draught power and role of cattle as cultural value were difficult to quantify, they were not considered while allocating environmental burden. The complexity involved in these functions is discussed separately under the discussion section of the present paper. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has honoured and worshiped in Indian culture. In Gujarat, slaughter of cattle (cow, calf, bull and bullock) is prohibited by law under the Gujarat Animal Preservation (Amendment) Act 2011. Slaughter of unfit animals such as she-buffalo (incapable of or ceased producing milk), buffalo bull and bullock (incapable

of breeding or working as draught animal) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old unproductive COWS and buffaloes were retained by the farmers. Surplus male calves from cows and buffaloes were neither reared nor sent for slaughter by the farmers. Such calves were let loose by the farmers in nearby villages. Therefore, meat was not considered as a co-product in the present assessment. The allocation procedure described by ISO 14044 (2006) states that, whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. When allocation cannot be avoided, the inputs and outputs of the system should be partitioned between its different products in a way that it reflects the underlying physical relationships among them, i.e. mass balance. Where physical relationship cannot be established, the inputs and outputs should be allocated among the products in a way that reflects other relationships among them; for example, inputs and outputs might be allocated among co- products in proportion to the economic value of the products. Allocation among co-products can also be undertaken using the biological method as recommended by IDF (2010), on the basis of the energy required to produce the amount of each co-product leaving the farm. In compliance with these rules, the mass, economic and digestibility allocations were applied only to those inputs in which human inedible crop by-products and residues were used for feeding of animals. Mass allocation was applied by computing the proportion of each feed by- product or residue in total quantity of biomass produced after considering harvesting losses. Economic allocation was applied on the basis of proportion of crop by-products or residues in total economic value of respective crop. For computing biological allocation, proportion of digestible DM of each feed ingredient was considered. The digestibility (%) of various feed ingredients was obtained from Feedipedia (2015). GHG emissions were
Fig. 2. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary (-) indicating process (-) and emission flows.

allocated between co-products (milk and manure) and other functions (finance and insurance) on the basis of their economic value. The details of allocation factors used in the present assessment are presented in Table 1.

Life cycle inventory encompasses all primary and secondary technical data related to the processes within the system boundary, e.g. details of animal husbandry, manure management, feed production, feed basket and products produced in dairy system. The primary data were collected directly from the farmers, whereas secondary data were obtained from peer-reviewed literature. The details of input data, along with their sources, are summarised in this section.

The economic value of bovines as an insurance for the household was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and the insurance premium, i.e. the cost that farmers would need to pay to purchase insurance coverage equal to the capital value of their herd. An insurance premium of 5% was applied for all farms after consultation with the national insurance company in India.

The most important GHGs related to smallholder dairy systems were CO2, methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions of CO2 resulted from the combustion of fossil fuel to power farm machinery and from electricity used in feed production and processing. The CO2 emissions from diesel were computed using the default emission factor (EF), as per IPCC (2006) guidelines. Soil carbon fluxes for on-farm and off- farm crops were excluded from the present assessment.

Methane was produced by ruminants mainly as a by-product of enteric fermentation. For estimating enteric CH4 emissions, average daily feed intake expressed as gross energy intake was calculated from the diet for cattle and buffalo. Enteric CH4 emissions were estimated as a function of intake, using the conversion factor (Ym = 6.5% of gross energy intake) and computing EF for each animal category (IPCC 2006, table 10.12, eqn 10.21). Methane emissions from manure were estimated as a proportion of the maximum CH4 potential (Bo) of manure volatile solids (VS) excreted (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.23). Estimated excretions of manure VS were obtained using the DM intake and diet digestibility (IPCC 2006; eqn 10.24). The proportion of Bo that was emitted from manure VS was computed using specific CH4 conversion factor (MCF) for solid manure storage system, i.e. 5.0% (IPCC 2006, table 10.17). The MCF was selected considering an annual average ambient temperature >28°C for Anand district.

Emissions of N2O from manure were derived after calculating nitrogen (N) excretion, which was estimated as the total N in feed intake minus the N in milk and that was retained in animals (calf and heifer growth). Direct N2O emissions from the stored manure were estimated using the corresponding default EF for solid manure storage system, i.e. 0.005 kg N2O-N/kg N excreted (IPCC 2006, table 10.21, eqn 10.25). For synthetic N fertiliser, the default EF was 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N applied. Volatilisation of NH3 and leaching of NO3 contributed to indirect emissions of N20. Losses of N through volatilisation (NH3 and NOX) and leaching from solid manure storage system were considered as 30% and 4%, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 10.22). The fraction of N that volatilised as NH3 and NO under different conditions was considered as 0.10 and 0.20 kg/kg of N from synthetic or organic fertilisers applied, respectively, and N loss by leaching was considered as 0.30 kg/kg of N applied to soil (IPCC 2006, table 11.3). Default EF for N2O emissions was 0.01 and 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg of N volatilised and leached, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 11.3).

The focus of the present LCA study was on impact category climate change. Total GHG emissions included CO2, CH4 and N2O from feed production (cultivation, processing and transport), CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N20 from manure management. The sum of GHG emissions was expressed in CO2-equivalents after considering the global warming potential of CO2 as 1, CH4 as 25 and N20 as 298 (IPCC 2007), which have been revised for CH4 as 28 and N2O as 265 (IPCC 2013).
14: The goal of the present LCA study was to calculate the CF of milk produced at smallholder dairy systems in Anand. Economic functions of the smallholder systems included in the present assessment were production of milk, manure and role of bovines as finance and insurance. The functional unit was '1 kg of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) produced at the farm-gate', expressed as kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-eq) per kilogram of FPCM. Total cow milk produced at the farm was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein, using the following FAO (2010) equation:
Buffalo milk production was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.1% protein using the following Di Palo (1992) equation:
FPCM (kg) = milk production (1 + 0.011 ((10 X fat (%) 40) + (10 X protein (%) 31))).
The scope of the LCA indicates the overall approach used to establish the system boundary, which determines which unit processes are included in the LCA and reflects the goal of the

Fig. 1. Location of selected villages within Anand district.

study (IDF 2010). In the present assessment, on-farm and off- farm processes related to the dairy production were analysed. On- farm processes were mostly the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy cattle, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuels. Off-farm processes included those related to the off-farm dairy activities, i.e. inputs involved in production, processing and transportation of external feeds. A 'cradle-to- farm gate' system boundary included all upstream processes in livestock production up to the point where the co-products (milk and manure) left the farm (Fig. 2). An attributional approach of LCA was applied in the present assessment to imply emissions under the current production scenario (Thomassen et al. 2008b). Allocation problem usually occurs when concentrate ingredients are part of multi-functional process and when the environmental burden is divided between milk and other products of the system. LCA has a product focus and the guidelines of LCA (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) provide rules on how to allocate the environmental impact of a process in the case of multiple outputs. In the present assessment, multiple functions of livestock (i.e. milk, manure, finance and insurance) were handled as multiple products of the production system. As the other functions of the system such as draught power and role of cattle as cultural value were difficult to quantify, they were not considered while allocating environmental burden. The complexity involved in these functions is discussed separately under the discussion section of the present paper. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has honoured and worshiped in Indian culture. In Gujarat, slaughter of cattle (cow, calf, bull and bullock) is prohibited by law under the Gujarat Animal Preservation (Amendment) Act 2011. Slaughter of unfit animals such as she-buffalo (incapable of or ceased producing milk), buffalo bull and bullock (incapable

of breeding or working as draught animal) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old unproductive COWS and buffaloes were retained by the farmers. Surplus male calves from cows and buffaloes were neither reared nor sent for slaughter by the farmers. Such calves were let loose by the farmers in nearby villages. Therefore, meat was not considered as a co-product in the present assessment. The allocation procedure described by ISO 14044 (2006) states that, whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. When allocation cannot be avoided, the inputs and outputs of the system should be partitioned between its different products in a way that it reflects the underlying physical relationships among them, i.e. mass balance. Where physical relationship cannot be established, the inputs and outputs should be allocated among the products in a way that reflects other relationships among them; for example, inputs and outputs might be allocated among co- products in proportion to the economic value of the products. Allocation among co-products can also be undertaken using the biological method as recommended by IDF (2010), on the basis of the energy required to produce the amount of each co-product leaving the farm. In compliance with these rules, the mass, economic and digestibility allocations were applied only to those inputs in which human inedible crop by-products and residues were used for feeding of animals. Mass allocation was applied by computing the proportion of each feed by- product or residue in total quantity of biomass produced after considering harvesting losses. Economic allocation was applied on the basis of proportion of crop by-products or residues in total economic value of respective crop. For computing biological allocation, proportion of digestible DM of each feed ingredient was considered. The digestibility (%) of various feed ingredients was obtained from Feedipedia (2015). GHG emissions were
Fig. 2. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary (-) indicating process (-) and emission flows.

allocated between co-products (milk and manure) and other functions (finance and insurance) on the basis of their economic value. The details of allocation factors used in the present assessment are presented in Table 1.

Life cycle inventory encompasses all primary and secondary technical data related to the processes within the system boundary, e.g. details of animal husbandry, manure management, feed production, feed basket and products produced in dairy system. The primary data were collected directly from the farmers, whereas secondary data were obtained from peer-reviewed literature. The details of input data, along with their sources, are summarised in this section.

In the LCA model, cows and buffaloes were divided into categories like lactating animals, replacement animals (heifers and pregnant females), retired animals (old unproductive) and oxen. Cows and buffaloes above 10 years of age and incapable of or ceased producing milk were considered as retired. The herd composition of each smallholder farm was obtained at the time of field visits. Heart girth of animals was measured during field visits and bodyweights were calculated as per Shaeffer's formula (Sastry et al. 1982; Garg et al. 2013), as follows:
Bodyweight (kg) = ((heart girth (cm)/2.54) X length of 2 the body (cm)/2.54)/300) X 0.4536).
Farmers were asked about annual average daily milk production of animals, and the quantities of milk consumed at home and sold
for processing were recorded. The average fat content and the price of milk were recorded from the receipts given by the dairy cooperative societies to the farmers after sale of milk.

For on-farm feed products, the details of crop-wise cultivation practices such as area under cultivation, application rate of synthetic fertilisers, manure, pesticides and seeds, yields of human edible crop products, inedible crop by-products and residues, use of draught power, tractor and fossil fuels were recorded through interviews. Details of off-farm feed products such as purchased cattle feed, by-products of concentrates and oilseed meals, along with their source, mode of transport and annual average price, were obtained from farmers. All off-farm feed products were purchased from various states of India. The composition of cattle feed was obtained from a local feed factory situated in Anand. To estimate the quantity of feed inputs at farm, feeding calendars were prepared after discussion with the farmers. These estimates were then translated into kg DM for each feed by applying literature-based DM conversion factors (Feedipedia 2015).

The quantities of manure produced, used as fertiliser for on- farm crops, sold as fertiliser and used as fuel in the form of dung cakes were obtained on the basis of information provided by farmers. Economic value of manure as a fertiliser was obtained from local market in each village (Scoones 1992). The nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium content of solid manure were considered as 0.5%, 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively (Pennington et al. 2009).
In smallholder system, animals could serve as means of finance to the farmers. Therefore, the role of bovines as finance was considered as the avoidance of paying an interest

The scope of the LCA indicates the overall approach used to establish the system boundary, which determines which unit processes are included in the LCA and reflects the goal of the

Fig. 1. Location of selected villages within Anand district.

study (IDF 2010). In the present assessment, on-farm and off- farm processes related to the dairy production were analysed. On- farm processes were mostly the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy cattle, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuels. Off-farm processes included those related to the off-farm dairy activities, i.e. inputs involved in production, processing and transportation of external feeds. A 'cradle-to- farm gate' system boundary included all upstream processes in livestock production up to the point where the co-products (milk and manure) left the farm (Fig. 2). An attributional approach of LCA was applied in the present assessment to imply emissions under the current production scenario (Thomassen et al. 2008b). Allocation problem usually occurs when concentrate ingredients are part of multi-functional process and when the environmental burden is divided between milk and other products of the system. LCA has a product focus and the guidelines of LCA (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) provide rules on how to allocate the environmental impact of a process in the case of multiple outputs. In the present assessment, multiple functions of livestock (i.e. milk, manure, finance and insurance) were handled as multiple products of the production system. As the other functions of the system such as draught power and role of cattle as cultural value were difficult to quantify, they were not considered while allocating environmental burden. The complexity involved in these functions is discussed separately under the discussion section of the present paper. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has honoured and worshiped in Indian culture. In Gujarat, slaughter of cattle (cow, calf, bull and bullock) is prohibited by law under the Gujarat Animal Preservation (Amendment) Act 2011. Slaughter of unfit animals such as she-buffalo (incapable of or ceased producing milk), buffalo bull and bullock (incapable

of breeding or working as draught animal) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old unproductive COWS and buffaloes were retained by the farmers. Surplus male calves from cows and buffaloes were neither reared nor sent for slaughter by the farmers. Such calves were let loose by the farmers in nearby villages. Therefore, meat was not considered as a co-product in the present assessment. The allocation procedure described by ISO 14044 (2006) states that, whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. When allocation cannot be avoided, the inputs and outputs of the system should be partitioned between its different products in a way that it reflects the underlying physical relationships among them, i.e. mass balance. Where physical relationship cannot be established, the inputs and outputs should be allocated among the products in a way that reflects other relationships among them; for example, inputs and outputs might be allocated among co- products in proportion to the economic value of the products. Allocation among co-products can also be undertaken using the biological method as recommended by IDF (2010), on the basis of the energy required to produce the amount of each co-product leaving the farm. In compliance with these rules, the mass, economic and digestibility allocations were applied only to those inputs in which human inedible crop by-products and residues were used for feeding of animals. Mass allocation was applied by computing the proportion of each feed by- product or residue in total quantity of biomass produced after considering harvesting losses. Economic allocation was applied on the basis of proportion of crop by-products or residues in total economic value of respective crop. For computing biological allocation, proportion of digestible DM of each feed ingredient was considered. The digestibility (%) of various feed ingredients was obtained from Feedipedia (2015). GHG emissions were
Fig. 2. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary (-) indicating process (-) and emission flows.

allocated between co-products (milk and manure) and other functions (finance and insurance) on the basis of their economic value. The details of allocation factors used in the present assessment are presented in Table 1.

Life cycle inventory encompasses all primary and secondary technical data related to the processes within the system boundary, e.g. details of animal husbandry, manure management, feed production, feed basket and products produced in dairy system. The primary data were collected directly from the farmers, whereas secondary data were obtained from peer-reviewed literature. The details of input data, along with their sources, are summarised in this section.

In the LCA model, cows and buffaloes were divided into categories like lactating animals, replacement animals (heifers and pregnant females), retired animals (old unproductive) and oxen. Cows and buffaloes above 10 years of age and incapable of or ceased producing milk were considered as retired. The herd composition of each smallholder farm was obtained at the time of field visits. Heart girth of animals was measured during field visits and bodyweights were calculated as per Shaeffer's formula (Sastry et al. 1982; Garg et al. 2013), as follows:
Bodyweight (kg) = ((heart girth (cm)/2.54) X length of 2 the body (cm)/2.54)/300) X 0.4536).
Farmers were asked about annual average daily milk production of animals, and the quantities of milk consumed at home and sold
for processing were recorded. The average fat content and the price of milk were recorded from the receipts given by the dairy cooperative societies to the farmers after sale of milk.

For on-farm feed products, the details of crop-wise cultivation practices such as area under cultivation, application rate of synthetic fertilisers, manure, pesticides and seeds, yields of human edible crop products, inedible crop by-products and residues, use of draught power, tractor and fossil fuels were recorded through interviews. Details of off-farm feed products such as purchased cattle feed, by-products of concentrates and oilseed meals, along with their source, mode of transport and annual average price, were obtained from farmers. All off-farm feed products were purchased from various states of India. The composition of cattle feed was obtained from a local feed factory situated in Anand. To estimate the quantity of feed inputs at farm, feeding calendars were prepared after discussion with the farmers. These estimates were then translated into kg DM for each feed by applying literature-based DM conversion factors (Feedipedia 2015).

The quantities of manure produced, used as fertiliser for on- farm crops, sold as fertiliser and used as fuel in the form of dung cakes were obtained on the basis of information provided by farmers. Economic value of manure as a fertiliser was obtained from local market in each village (Scoones 1992). The nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium content of solid manure were considered as 0.5%, 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively (Pennington et al. 2009).
In smallholder system, animals could serve as means of finance to the farmers. Therefore, the role of bovines as finance was considered as the avoidance of paying an interest
Table 1. Allocation factors (%) used for feed inputs and co-products produced at farms

Parameter|Mass allocation|Economic allocation|Digestibility allocation Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs Pearl millet grain|20.4|31.1|30.9 Maize grain|39.5|47.4|51.7 Maize gluten meal|2.2|3.2|3.0 Maize bran|7.3|12.1|7.8 Rice polish (16% oil)|1.7|1.6|2.3 Rice bran|4.2|4.8|5.2 De-oiled rice bran|3.6|1.7|4.2 Wheat bran|5.5|3.9|6.2 Soybean chunni|42.2|49.1|51.2 Pigeon pea chunni|3.1|3.7|3.7 Cottonseed meal|4.4|7.9|7.4 Guar meal|16.2|5.6|20.5 Maize germ meal|1.4|1.3|1.5 Rapeseed meal|34.7|28.7|43.5 Sorghum straw|65.0|36.7|52.9 Millet straw|71.4|52.0|57.6 Paddy straw|57.2|23.8|37.2 Wheat straw|49.7|20.2|35.9 Molasses|2.5|3.3|5.4 Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions Milk||78.8| Manure||4.2| Finance||12.5| Insurance||4.5|

by the farmers when borrowing money from a bank or an informal money lender (Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as finance was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and an interest rate on finance. Economic value of cattle and buffalo was obtained after consultation with insurance companies in the district. For all farms, an interest rate of 14% was applied, as it was the minimum interest rate on personal finance offered by the nationalised bank in India.

The insurance function of livestock was the opportunity to sell animals when needed and, thus, having animals was equal to having insurance (Moll 2005). The benefit of bovines as insurance was understood as the absence to pay a premium in case of insurance (Bebe et al. 2002; Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as an insurance for the household was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and the insurance premium, i.e. the cost that farmers would need to pay to purchase insurance coverage equal to the capital value of their herd. An insurance premium of 5% was applied for all farms after consultation with the national insurance company in India.

The most important GHGs related to smallholder dairy systems were CO2, methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions of CO2 resulted from the combustion of fossil fuel to power farm machinery and from electricity used in feed production and processing. The CO2 emissions from diesel were computed using the default emission factor (EF), as per IPCC (2006) guidelines. Soil carbon fluxes for on-farm and off- farm crops were excluded from the present assessment.

Methane was produced by ruminants mainly as a by-product of enteric fermentation. For estimating enteric CH4 emissions, average daily feed intake expressed as gross energy intake was calculated from the diet for cattle and buffalo. Enteric CH4 emissions were estimated as a function of intake, using the conversion factor (Ym = 6.5% of gross energy intake) and computing EF for each animal category (IPCC 2006, table 10.12, eqn 10.21). Methane emissions from manure were estimated as a proportion of the maximum CH4 potential (Bo) of manure volatile solids (VS) excreted (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.23). Estimated excretions of manure VS were obtained using the DM intake and diet digestibility (IPCC 2006; eqn 10.24). The proportion of Bo that was emitted from manure VS was computed using specific CH4 conversion factor (MCF) for solid manure storage system, i.e. 5.0% (IPCC 2006, table 10.17). The MCF was selected considering an annual average ambient temperature >28°C for Anand district.

Emissions of N2O from manure were derived after calculating nitrogen (N) excretion, which was estimated as the total N in feed intake minus the N in milk and that was retained in animals (calf and heifer growth). Direct N2O emissions from the stored manure were estimated using the corresponding default EF for solid manure storage system, i.e. 0.005 kg N2O-N/kg N excreted (IPCC 2006, table 10.21, eqn 10.25). For synthetic N fertiliser, the default EF was 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N applied. Volatilisation of NH3 and leaching of NO3 contributed to indirect emissions of N20. Losses of N through volatilisation (NH3 and NOX) and leaching from solid manure storage system were considered as 30% and 4%, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 10.22). The fraction of N that volatilised as NH3 and NO under different conditions was considered as 0.10 and 0.20 kg/kg of N from synthetic or organic fertilisers applied, respectively, and N loss by leaching was considered as 0.30 kg/kg of N applied to soil (IPCC 2006, table 11.3). Default EF for N2O emissions was 0.01 and 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg of N volatilised and leached, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 11.3).

The focus of the present LCA study was on impact category climate change. Total GHG emissions included CO2, CH4 and N2O from feed production (cultivation, processing and transport), CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N20 from manure management. The sum of GHG emissions was expressed in CO2-equivalents after considering the global warming potential of CO2 as 1, CH4 as 25 and N20 as 298 (IPCC 2007), which have been revised for CH4 as 28 and N2O as 265 (IPCC 2013).

allocated between co-products (milk and manure) and other functions (finance and insurance) on the basis of their economic value. The details of allocation factors used in the present assessment are presented in Table 1.

Life cycle inventory encompasses all primary and secondary technical data related to the processes within the system boundary, e.g. details of animal husbandry, manure management, feed production, feed basket and products produced in dairy system. The primary data were collected directly from the farmers, whereas secondary data were obtained from peer-reviewed literature. The details of input data, along with their sources, are summarised in this section.

In the LCA model, cows and buffaloes were divided into categories like lactating animals, replacement animals (heifers and pregnant females), retired animals (old unproductive) and oxen. Cows and buffaloes above 10 years of age and incapable of or ceased producing milk were considered as retired. The herd composition of each smallholder farm was obtained at the time of field visits. Heart girth of animals was measured during field visits and bodyweights were calculated as per Shaeffer's formula (Sastry et al. 1982; Garg et al. 2013), as follows:
Bodyweight (kg) = ((heart girth (cm)/2.54) X length of 2 the body (cm)/2.54)/300) X 0.4536).
Farmers were asked about annual average daily milk production of animals, and the quantities of milk consumed at home and sold
for processing were recorded. The average fat content and the price of milk were recorded from the receipts given by the dairy cooperative societies to the farmers after sale of milk.

For on-farm feed products, the details of crop-wise cultivation practices such as area under cultivation, application rate of synthetic fertilisers, manure, pesticides and seeds, yields of human edible crop products, inedible crop by-products and residues, use of draught power, tractor and fossil fuels were recorded through interviews. Details of off-farm feed products such as purchased cattle feed, by-products of concentrates and oilseed meals, along with their source, mode of transport and annual average price, were obtained from farmers. All off-farm feed products were purchased from various states of India. The composition of cattle feed was obtained from a local feed factory situated in Anand. To estimate the quantity of feed inputs at farm, feeding calendars were prepared after discussion with the farmers. These estimates were then translated into kg DM for each feed by applying literature-based DM conversion factors (Feedipedia 2015).

The quantities of manure produced, used as fertiliser for on- farm crops, sold as fertiliser and used as fuel in the form of dung cakes were obtained on the basis of information provided by farmers. Economic value of manure as a fertiliser was obtained from local market in each village (Scoones 1992). The nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium content of solid manure were considered as 0.5%, 0.3% and 0.5%, respectively (Pennington et al. 2009).
In smallholder system, animals could serve as means of finance to the farmers. Therefore, the role of bovines as finance was considered as the avoidance of paying an interest
Table 1. Allocation factors (%) used for feed inputs and co-products produced at farms

Parameter|Mass allocation|Economic allocation|Digestibility allocation Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs|Feed inputs Pearl millet grain|20.4|31.1|30.9 Maize grain|39.5|47.4|51.7 Maize gluten meal|2.2|3.2|3.0 Maize bran|7.3|12.1|7.8 Rice polish (16% oil)|1.7|1.6|2.3 Rice bran|4.2|4.8|5.2 De-oiled rice bran|3.6|1.7|4.2 Wheat bran|5.5|3.9|6.2 Soybean chunni|42.2|49.1|51.2 Pigeon pea chunni|3.1|3.7|3.7 Cottonseed meal|4.4|7.9|7.4 Guar meal|16.2|5.6|20.5 Maize germ meal|1.4|1.3|1.5 Rapeseed meal|34.7|28.7|43.5 Sorghum straw|65.0|36.7|52.9 Millet straw|71.4|52.0|57.6 Paddy straw|57.2|23.8|37.2 Wheat straw|49.7|20.2|35.9 Molasses|2.5|3.3|5.4 Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions|Co-products or economic functions Milk||78.8| Manure||4.2| Finance||12.5| Insurance||4.5|

by the farmers when borrowing money from a bank or an informal money lender (Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as finance was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and an interest rate on finance. Economic value of cattle and buffalo was obtained after consultation with insurance companies in the district. For all farms, an interest rate of 14% was applied, as it was the minimum interest rate on personal finance offered by the nationalised bank in India.

The insurance function of livestock was the opportunity to sell animals when needed and, thus, having animals was equal to having insurance (Moll 2005). The benefit of bovines as insurance was understood as the absence to pay a premium in case of insurance (Bebe et al. 2002; Moll et al. 2007; Behnke and Muthami 2011). The economic value of bovines as an insurance for the household was calculated as a product of economic value of herd and the insurance premium, i.e. the cost that farmers would need to pay to purchase insurance coverage equal to the capital value of their herd. An insurance premium of 5% was applied for all farms after consultation with the national insurance company in India.

The most important GHGs related to smallholder dairy systems were CO2, methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). Emissions of CO2 resulted from the combustion of fossil fuel to power farm machinery and from electricity used in feed production and processing. The CO2 emissions from diesel were computed using the default emission factor (EF), as per IPCC (2006) guidelines. Soil carbon fluxes for on-farm and off- farm crops were excluded from the present assessment.

Methane was produced by ruminants mainly as a by-product of enteric fermentation. For estimating enteric CH4 emissions, average daily feed intake expressed as gross energy intake was calculated from the diet for cattle and buffalo. Enteric CH4 emissions were estimated as a function of intake, using the conversion factor (Ym = 6.5% of gross energy intake) and computing EF for each animal category (IPCC 2006, table 10.12, eqn 10.21). Methane emissions from manure were estimated as a proportion of the maximum CH4 potential (Bo) of manure volatile solids (VS) excreted (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.23). Estimated excretions of manure VS were obtained using the DM intake and diet digestibility (IPCC 2006; eqn 10.24). The proportion of Bo that was emitted from manure VS was computed using specific CH4 conversion factor (MCF) for solid manure storage system, i.e. 5.0% (IPCC 2006, table 10.17). The MCF was selected considering an annual average ambient temperature >28°C for Anand district.

Emissions of N2O from manure were derived after calculating nitrogen (N) excretion, which was estimated as the total N in feed intake minus the N in milk and that was retained in animals (calf and heifer growth). Direct N2O emissions from the stored manure were estimated using the corresponding default EF for solid manure storage system, i.e. 0.005 kg N2O-N/kg N excreted (IPCC 2006, table 10.21, eqn 10.25). For synthetic N fertiliser, the default EF was 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N applied. Volatilisation of NH3 and leaching of NO3 contributed to indirect emissions of N20. Losses of N through volatilisation (NH3 and NOX) and leaching from solid manure storage system were considered as 30% and 4%, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 10.22). The fraction of N that volatilised as NH3 and NO under different conditions was considered as 0.10 and 0.20 kg/kg of N from synthetic or organic fertilisers applied, respectively, and N loss by leaching was considered as 0.30 kg/kg of N applied to soil (IPCC 2006, table 11.3). Default EF for N2O emissions was 0.01 and 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg of N volatilised and leached, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 11.3).

The focus of the present LCA study was on impact category climate change. Total GHG emissions included CO2, CH4 and N2O from feed production (cultivation, processing and transport), CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N20 from manure management. The sum of GHG emissions was expressed in CO2-equivalents after considering the global warming potential of CO2 as 1, CH4 as 25 and N20 as 298 (IPCC 2007), which have been revised for CH4 as 28 and N2O as 265 (IPCC 2013).
19: The goal of the present LCA study was to calculate the CF of milk produced at smallholder dairy systems in Anand. Economic functions of the smallholder systems included in the present assessment were production of milk, manure and role of bovines as finance and insurance. The functional unit was '1 kg of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) produced at the farm-gate', expressed as kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2-eq) per kilogram of FPCM. Total cow milk produced at the farm was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein, using the following FAO (2010) equation:
Buffalo milk production was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.1% protein using the following Di Palo (1992) equation:
FPCM (kg) = milk production (1 + 0.011 ((10 X fat (%) 40) + (10 X protein (%) 31))).
The scope of the LCA indicates the overall approach used to establish the system boundary, which determines which unit processes are included in the LCA and reflects the goal of the

"Gujarat Anand Umreth N Khambhat Tarapur [ ] Sojitra Petlad [X] Borsad [X] [X] Anand [ ] Ankla [ ]"| "Gujarat Anand Umreth N Khambhat Tarapur [ ] Sojitra Petlad [X] Borsad [X] [X] Anand [ ] Ankla [ ]"|Village [ ] Ashi [ ] Asodar [X] Dahewan [ ] Davol [ ] Gopalpura [ ] Lunej [X] Navakhal [X] Sandesar [X] Shekhadi [ ] Sundalpura [ ] Thamna [X] Vatadara [ ] Taluka

study (IDF 2010). In the present assessment, on-farm and off- farm processes related to the dairy production were analysed. On- farm processes were mostly the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy cattle, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuels. Off-farm processes included those related to the off-farm dairy activities, i.e. inputs involved in production, processing and transportation of external feeds. A 'cradle-to- farm gate' system boundary included all upstream processes in livestock production up to the point where the co-products (milk and manure) left the farm (Fig. 2). An attributional approach of LCA was applied in the present assessment to imply emissions under the current production scenario (Thomassen et al. 2008b). Allocation problem usually occurs when concentrate ingredients are part of multi-functional process and when the environmental burden is divided between milk and other products of the system. LCA has a product focus and the guidelines of LCA (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) provide rules on how to allocate the environmental impact of a process in the case of multiple outputs. In the present assessment, multiple functions of livestock (i.e. milk, manure, finance and insurance) were handled as multiple products of the production system. As the other functions of the system such as draught power and role of cattle as cultural value were difficult to quantify, they were not considered while allocating environmental burden. The complexity involved in these functions is discussed separately under the discussion section of the present paper. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has honoured and worshiped in Indian culture. In Gujarat, slaughter of cattle (cow, calf, bull and bullock) is prohibited by law under the Gujarat Animal Preservation (Amendment) Act 2011. Slaughter of unfit animals such as she-buffalo (incapable of or ceased producing milk), buffalo bull and bullock (incapable

of breeding or working as draught animal) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old unproductive COWS and buffaloes were retained by the farmers. Surplus male calves from cows and buffaloes were neither reared nor sent for slaughter by the farmers. Such calves were let loose by the farmers in nearby villages. Therefore, meat was not considered as a co-product in the present assessment. The allocation procedure described by ISO 14044 (2006) states that, whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. When allocation cannot be avoided, the inputs and outputs of the system should be partitioned between its different products in a way that it reflects the underlying physical relationships among them, i.e. mass balance. Where physical relationship cannot be established, the inputs and outputs should be allocated among the products in a way that reflects other relationships among them; for example, inputs and outputs might be allocated among co- products in proportion to the economic value of the products. Allocation among co-products can also be undertaken using the biological method as recommended by IDF (2010), on the basis of the energy required to produce the amount of each co-product leaving the farm. In compliance with these rules, the mass, economic and digestibility allocations were applied only to those inputs in which human inedible crop by-products and residues were used for feeding of animals. Mass allocation was applied by computing the proportion of each feed by- product or residue in total quantity of biomass produced after considering harvesting losses. Economic allocation was applied on the basis of proportion of crop by-products or residues in total economic value of respective crop. For computing biological allocation, proportion of digestible DM of each feed ingredient was considered. The digestibility (%) of various feed ingredients was obtained from Feedipedia (2015).
