herd size, bodyweight, AFC and milk production performance were in agreement with those in earlier reports (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Pundir 2002; Singh and Barwal 2010). A wide range of stocking rate (1-51 adult cattle unit/ha) was reported in semiarid regions of India (Shankar and Gupta 1992), which agreed with our findings on stocking rate in the study area. Similar to present findings, ~10-39% of the cow dung was reported to be utilised as fuel (Valbuena et al. 2011), and varying proportions of dung as manure in Indian villages (Thirunavukkarasu et al. 2014). The farmers in Anand district used less fossil fuel for on-farm crop production than did those in European farms (O'Brien et al. 2014; 97.0 VS 14.8 L/ha).

Smallholders in tropical regions of the world rear bovines for multiple functions (Moll et al. 2007; Weiler et al. 2014); therefore, we emphasise that all functions should be considered while handling environmental burden in smallholder production system. In such system, the various ways that livestock serve their owners could be stated as products or in LCA terms, co- products, which are one or more products coming from same the production system (ISO 14044 2006). Multiple functions of livestock could be grouped as marketable (i.e. those having direct or indirect economic value) and non-marketable functions (i.e. those that are difficult to quantify in economic terms). Marketable functions with direct economic value include milk and manure, and provide regular economic returns to farmers. For example, smallholder farmers in Anand district earned daily economic returns from sale of milk and quarterly economic returns from sale of manure. Functions with indirect market value (finance and insurance) provide seldom economic returns to farmers; nevertheless, their importance for smallholder households with limited financial ability cannot be ignored. Farmers in our study area were ready to sell their animals in situations of urgent cash requirement, such as, for example, meeting medical expenses, purchasing land and function like marriage. Bebe et al. (2002) and Weiler et al. (2014) also reported similar situations in which Kenyan smallholder farmers exchanged their animals to cash.

Non-marketable functions include role of cattle as drought power and socio-cultural values. We observed that the use of cattle as draught power was an individual preference of the farmers. In the present study, only one farmer used ox for traction; however, the same farmer also used a tractor for farm operations. In spite of low average land holding, the majority of the smallholders in study area hired farm machinery from local villages. This result reflects increasing mechanisation of farming and declining trend of utilisation of animal power in agriculture with respect to the present study area. Ray et al. (1996) and

Valbuena et al. (2011) also reported similar trend of increasing mechanisation and decreasing utility of draft animals in agriculture. It is also important to note that the current attributional approach of LCA would be inadequate to fully account draught function of the smallholder system because it would not tell us about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of fossil fuel required to power farm machinery. Therefore, in such situation, applying consequential approach of LCA would be more appropriate.

Socio-cultural value of bovines was another non-marketable function of the smallholder system. In India, cow rearing is a part of peoples' culture and, many a times, socio-cultural value of bovines is much more important to the farmers than are any other functions. In fact, for all farmers in the study area, bovines were part of their socio-cultural values. We observed that 4 of 60 households in the study area retained six retired animals (two COWS and four buffaloes). In addition to cultural values of bovines, existing anti-slaughter laws in Gujarat could be another reason for retaining unproductive stock by the smallholder farmers. This aspect of smallholder production system in India needs to be explored further before considering socio-cultural value as a function for allocation of GHG emissions. Due to complexity involved in non-marketable functions (draught power and socio- cultural value), they were not considered while allocating GHG emissions in the present study.

Previous study on Kenyan smallholder farms considered milk, meat, finance and insurance under economic allocation and socio- cultural values under livelihood allocation on the basis of farmer perception, by conducting a ranking exercise (Weiler et al. 2014). However, they also reported that only 45% of the farmers in their study area could rank multiple functions of cattle as farmers found ranking exercise outside their scope. In addition, an economic quantification of intangible function such as socio-cultural value was unlikely to meaningfully cover all benefits of cattle rearing to the farmers. Therefore, further research is required for developing methodology for precise quantification of non- marketable functions of bovines and using them for allocating GHG emissions from multi-functional smallholder systems. An economic quantification of marketable and non-marketable functions was applied in dairy production (Bebe et al. 2002); however, whether it covers all contributions of livestock to livelihoods is debatable. For instance, in present study, total economic value of milk includes the value of milk consumed by households plus the surplus milk that was sold for processing. However, an economic-allocation approach in LCA does not quantify nutritional benefits that the milk has provided to the household (Behnke and Muthami 2011).

We found that 30.9-67.8% of the manure was utilised either on- farm or off-farm for its fertiliser value, similar to Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). While computing economic allocation to co- products, economic value of total manure produced on farm (irrespective of its utilisation) was considered. In such circumstances, even if we performed system expansion or used consequential LCA, it would tell us only about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of synthetic fertilisers. In either approach of LCA, the positive impacts that manure has on soil structure, soil organic matter and water holding capacity still remain unexplored. These positive impacts could be an integral part of sustainable nutrient cycle in mixed crop-livestock system (Weiler et al. 2014). Nevertheless, accounting these
positive effects of manure in economic terms is highly complex and challenging, particularly in a multi-functional smallholder production system.
GHG emissions and hotspots

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district was characterised by a mixed crop-livestock system in which an average of 43.0% DM fed to the animals was from crop residues, 18.1% from human-inedible crop by-products, 20.2% from green fodder and 18.7% from processed cattle feed. On average, each farm emitted a total of 10 609.8 kg CO2-eq/year (digestibility allocation basis), of which ~69.3% of emissions were associated with CH4 from enteric fermentation. The higher proportion of CH4 was mostly related to the nature of ruminant digestion and was influenced by the quality of feed (Garg et al. 2013). In our study, an average CH4 emission factor for enteric fermentation was 72.3 and 77.9 kg/year for lactating cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas the feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Therefore, lower digestibility of feed might be associated with higher CH4 emissions due to enteric fermentation (Mangino et al. 2003). The other important aspect influencing total CH4 emissions at farm was the number of non-lactating animals that contributed to the total CH4 emissions. In majority of the smallholder farms, non-lactating animals were mostly the replacement cows and buffaloes, whereas in a few farms, in addition to replacement animals, retired animals and oxen were also present. Although non-lactating animals emitted less CH4 than did lactating animals, from the point of view of production of a single commodity, it is inefficient to keep animals that do not produce milk. However, this is where the importance of valuing multi-functionality begins to have a great effect on CF of milk production. Acknowledging multi-functionality of smallholder farms enabled us to consider non-lactating animals as still being productive, through serving other functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) than producing milk. In the present study, it was difficult to subcategorise young stock into suckling and non- suckling calves, and to obtain exact ages of young stock from farmers was also challenging. Due to the fact that young calves (i.e. less than 1 year of age) produce very limited amounts of CH4 (Singh et al. 2012), we excluded them from the present assessment. Next to enteric CH4, emissions of CO2, CH4 and N20 from feed production together contributed 17.3% to the total GHG emissions of farm. Within farm, GHG emissions from feed production for each category of animal depend on the type of feeds used in the ration of animal. For instance, the higher CO2 emissions for lactating and replacement animals were mainly attributed to feeding of processed feeds (i.e. cattle feed and oilseed meals) and the associated use of fertilisers, pesticides and fossil fuel during feed production. Use of processed feeds for feeding of retired animals was uncommon and this explains the lower feed-related CO2 emissions for these animals. In retired buffaloes, ~51.4% of the feed-related emissions were from CH4, the reason being the use of paddy straw (34-60% in total DM intake) for feeding of these animals. Paddy cultivation is well known for its relatively large CH4 emissions (Phong et al. 2011). For all categories of animals, the emissions of CH4 during feed production were associated with inclusion of crop by-products, i.e. de-oiled rice bran (29.5%) and rice polish (8.0%) in the

concentrate mixture and crop residue (i.e. paddy straw) in the rations. Farmers in the study area managed manure mostly in solid form; therefore, direct and indirect emissions of N20 from manure management had only a small share (<8.1%) in total GHG emissions of the farm. The N20 emissions contributed between 23.0% and 39.8% to the total GHG emissions from feed production and were attributed to application of nitrogenous fertilisers during cultivation. CF of milk production The weighted mean CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production of 60 smallholder farms in Anand was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. In the studied production system, manure had an economic importance for the farmers and, therefore, after applying co-product allocation to manure and other economic functions (finance and insurance), the CF of milk decreased by 22.7% (i.e. 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Similar pattern was also observed for CF of cow and buffalo milk when studied separately. This indicated the role of other economic functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) in sharing environmental burden of milk production in a multi-functional smallholder system. There has been limited research on CF of milk production in smallholder dairy systems. Weiler et al. (2014) reported that CF of milk production in Kenyan smallholder farms was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when only milk and meat were considered as co-products of the system. The CF of milk was further reduced to 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when economic function allocation (including milk, meat, manure, finance and insurance) was applied. Other studies on smallholder dairy systems in Kenya and Ethiopia also reported that CF of milk was changed to 1.5 and 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, respectively, when economic allocation was applied (Modupeore 2011; Woldegebriel 2013). Our findings of CF of milk production are in agreement with Modupeore (2011), Woldegebriel (2013) and Weiler et al. (2014). The CF of energy-corrected milk (ECM) in low productive dairy system of Andean highlands, Peru, was 13.8 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Bartl et al. 2011). The CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) in European countries was 1.6 for The Netherlands (Thomassen et al. 2008b) and 1.1 for Ireland (O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas in Swedish farms, it was 1.1 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Henriksson et al. 2011). The variation in CF of milk production in high- producing dairy systems was reported from 0.8 to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM in Ireland and from 0.8 to 1.3 kg CO2-eq/kg milk in Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) countries (de Vries and de Boer 2010). The annual FPCM yield in European high-producing specialised systems varied from 5380 to 8081 kg/cow (Thomassen et al. 2008b; O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas, in our case, it ranged from 715 to 3925 and from 842 to 4010 kg in cows and buffaloes, respectively. Thus, farms in Anand district produced CF in the range of 0.9-6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. For cow and buffalo milk, cradle-to-farm gate global average emission intensity in mixed system was 2.6 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, whereas in southern Asia, it was 5.5 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, respectively (Gerber et al. 2013). Our findings of CF values of milk production contradict the CF values reported in global studies by FAO (Gerber et al. 2013). Plausible explanations|for this could be the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by FAO. Global studies by FAO have considered N2O emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our study area were stall fed and housed in low-input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk, instead the milk was immediately carried to the village-level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N2O from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in Anand district. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (FAO 2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and Anand district. An estimated average feed digestibility used by FAO (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas, in the present study, feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher CF of milk. Another explanation for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be that an average milk production of cow and buffalo in our study area was 2007 and 1324 kg/year, respectively, whereas FAO (2010) reported an overall annual average of <1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk production levels in global studies used FAO statistical data, which were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014). The relationship between CF and milk output per lactating animal indicted that, with increasing yields, GHG emissions on a lactating-animal basis increased but decreased per kilogram of output (FPCM). Gerber et al. (2011) and Tubiello et al. (2012) also reported a similar relationship between productivity and GHG emissions of dairy production. Gerber et al. (2011) concluded that increasing productivity could be a viable mitigation approach, especially in areas where milk yields are currently below 2000 kg/year.cow. Therefore, in the present study, the possibility for reducing milk CF exists if productivity of animals producing less than 2000 kg milk/year is enhanced. We observed that as the feed digestibility increased, the milk CF decreased per unit of FPCM, with a moderate relationship between them. Lower feed digestibility was mainly attributed to the higher proportion of crop residues (up to 43.0%) in total DM intake of animals. Feed digestibility is among one of the factors influencing enteric CH4 emissions in farm animals (Wilkerson et al. 1994) and low levels (<55%) are often associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions per unit of product (FAO 2000). However, as feed digestibility and production levels increase,

CH4 emissions per unit of product decrease (Boadi et al. 2004). As evident from the present study, enteric CH4 was the major contributor (69.3%) to the total GHG emissions of farms. Therefore, as the feed digestibility increased, total GHG emissions (mainly enteric CH4) decreased, resulting in lower CF per unit of milk.
Mitigation options and implications for policy makers

Results of our study indicated that the CF of milk changes depending on the methodological choice of allocation within LCA. Common mitigation options focus on increased animal productivity through feeding nutritionally balanced rations (Garg et al. 2013) and better manure management (Herrero et al. 2010; Gerber et al. 2011). From the single-commodity point of view, i.e. milk, keeping a lower number of high- producing animals and replacing synthetic fertilisers with manure could result in higher milk production and lower CF of milk. Considering multi-functionality of a smallholder system, reducing the number of animals does not make sense because all animals play important roles and offer economic returns to the farmer. The most applicable mitigation options include improving feed digestibility, enhancing FCE and productivity of animals by feeding nutritionally balancedration (Garg al. 2013), reducing use of synthetic N-based fertilisers, improving efficiency of fertiliser application in crop production (Tirado et al. 2010) and covering the manure heaps with banana leaves to avoid nutrient loss during storage (Gichangi et al. 2007). Another mitigation option that lies outside the system boundary could be the use of biogas as a replacement to dung cakes (Pathak et al. 2009; Sharma and Nema 2013). These mitigation options could be effectively implemented through doorstep extension services to the farmers.

From the point of view of a smallholder farmer, the multiple functions of livestock were the main reasons behind keeping the animals. Contrary to mixed systems, the production of milk is by far the most important reason to keep dairy animals in specialised systems. Due to different nature of both systems, they should not be analysed in a same way, i.e. production units of LCA of specialised systems do not fit to multi-functional mixed systems. The LCA being an industrial tool, when applied to a smallholder dairy system, it poorly grasps the multi-functionality aspect. ISO 14044 (2006) also recommended that comparison among systems should be made on the basis of same functions, quantified by the same functional unit. Thus, a direct comparison of milk CF from dairy systems characterised by different degrees of specialisation is not appropriate if the systems differ in their output. Therefore, in future LCAs on smallholder systems, a serious consideration should be given to multi-functionality aspect. We emphasise that before comparing with smallholder systems, specialised farms would also need to be evaluated considering multi-functionality aspect.

utilisation data when expressed for 60 farms indicated that an average 67.8% of the produced manure was utilised as fertiliser for on-farm crops, 30.9% of the manure was sold as fertiliser (mostly by landless farmers and those who produced surplus manure on farm) and remaining 1.3% was used as fuel. Manure was applied to on-farm crops at an average application rate 70.3 kg N/ha.year. In 8 of 60 farms, an average 10% of the produced manure (290 kg DM/year) was mixed with left-over crop residues to prepare dung cakes, which were then used as replacement to firewood throughout the year. The dung cakes were usually prepared during summer and used as and when required by the households. Besides manure, urea was the most commonly used nitrogenous fertiliser by the farmers. Overall, synthetic fertiliser was applied at 145.7 kg N/ha.year.
GHG emissions of smallholder dairy production

Total GHG emissions and emission hotspots are presented in Table 4. Total GHG emissions after mass, economic and digestibility allocations averaged 11744.2, 9883.1 and 10609.8 kg CO2-eq/year.farm, respectively. On digestibility allocation, the major emission hotspot was methane produced from enteric fermentation (69.3%), followed by GHG emissions produced during feed production (17.3%). GHG emissions during feed production occurred due to cultivation, production, processing and transportation of off-farm and on-farm feeds. Emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O from cattle contributed 11.0%, 75.4% and 13.6%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions. The contribution of CO2, CH4 and N2O from buffalo was 8.2%, 80.5% and 11.3%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions of farms. Thus, enteric CH4 was the highest contributing GHG in total farm emissions.

Within herd, emission hotspots for different categories of animals based on digestibility allocation are presented in Fig. 3. In all categories of animals, CH4 emission from enteric fermentation (66.8-78.7%) and CO2, CH4 and N2O emissions from feed production (8.2-19.7%) were the two major hotspots. GHG emissions from manure management contributed least (less than 8.1%) to the total emissions from each category of animal. Retired (old unproductive) cows and buffalos emitted
an average of 1571.3 and 2556.1 kg CO2-eq/retirement year, respectively. GHG emissions from oxen were 3022.1 CO2-eq/ year.

The combined GHG emissions from off-farm and on-farm feed production (Fig. indicated that CO2 was the major hotspot (41.4-55.1%) in total feed-related GHG emissions for lactating and replacement animals. For retired animals, comparatively lower CO2 emissions were recorded than for any other category of animals. In case of retired buffaloes, CH4 was the highest contributing GHG (51.4%) to the total emissions from feed production. The contributions of CO2, CH4 and N2O to the total GHG emissions for oxen were 35.3%, 24.9% and 39.8%, respectively.
CF of milk production using different allocation methods

The CF of milk production under smallholder production system is presented in Table 5. The results of CF of milk production differed depending on the allocation method applied. When all the GHG emissions were allocated to a single product, i.e. 100% allocation to milk, the CF of cow milk was 2.3, 1.9 and 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM on mass, economic and digestibility basis, respectively; whereas, for buffalo milk, the CF was 3.0, 2.5 and 2.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM, respectively. When the milk, manure, finance and insurance were considered as economic functions of the smallholder system, average 78.8% of emissions were allocated to milk and remaining were allocated to other functions (i.e. 4.2% to manure, 12.5% to finance and 4.5% to insurance). Thus, the CF of milk based on economic allocation to co-products or functions was 1.6 and 1.9 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM in COWS and buffaloes, respectively. Figure 5 indicates across the 60 dairy farms that there was significant variability in CF of milk among farms, ranging from 0.9 to 6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. Overall, the mean CF of milk to the farm-gate, weighted by farm milk production was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, which reduced to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM when economic allocation to co-products or functions was applied.

Relationship between CF and feed digestibility

The relationship between milk CF and feed digestibility is presented in Fig. 7. It was observed that with increasing digestibility of rations, the GHG emissions decreased per unit of FPCM, indicating a moderate relationship (R2 = 0.30) between
feed digestibility and CF of milk. For farms in the study area, an average feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%.
Discussion
Smallholder farming system

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district represents a mixed crop-livestock system, in which farmers rely on crop by- products and residues for livestock feeding. The average age of livestock owners in the present study area was in line with Ravikumar et al. (2007) and Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). The average farm land owned by smallholders in Gujarat state was reported between 0.7 and 1.6 ha/household (Staal et al. 2006), which agreed with our findings on land holdings. Information on

herd size, bodyweight, AFC and milk production performance were in agreement with those in earlier reports (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Pundir 2002; Singh and Barwal 2010). A wide range of stocking rate (1-51 adult cattle unit/ha) was reported in semiarid regions of India (Shankar and Gupta 1992), which agreed with our findings on stocking rate in the study area. Similar to present findings, ~10-39% of the cow dung was reported to be utilised as fuel (Valbuena et al. 2011), and varying proportions of dung as manure in Indian villages (Thirunavukkarasu et al. 2014). The farmers in Anand district used less fossil fuel for on-farm crop production than did those in European farms (O'Brien et al. 2014; 97.0 VS 14.8 L/ha).

Smallholders in tropical regions of the world rear bovines for multiple functions (Moll et al. 2007; Weiler et al. 2014); therefore, we emphasise that all functions should be considered while handling environmental burden in smallholder production system. In such system, the various ways that livestock serve their owners could be stated as products or in LCA terms, co- products, which are one or more products coming from same the production system (ISO 14044 2006). Multiple functions of livestock could be grouped as marketable (i.e. those having direct or indirect economic value) and non-marketable functions (i.e. those that are difficult to quantify in economic terms). Marketable functions with direct economic value include milk and manure, and provide regular economic returns to farmers. For example, smallholder farmers in Anand district earned daily economic returns from sale of milk and quarterly economic returns from sale of manure. Functions with indirect market value (finance and insurance) provide seldom economic returns to farmers; nevertheless, their importance for smallholder households with limited financial ability cannot be ignored. Farmers in our study area were ready to sell their animals in situations of urgent cash requirement, such as, for example, meeting medical expenses, purchasing land and function like marriage. Bebe et al. (2002) and Weiler et al. (2014) also reported similar situations in which Kenyan smallholder farmers exchanged their animals to cash.

Non-marketable functions include role of cattle as drought power and socio-cultural values. We observed that the use of cattle as draught power was an individual preference of the farmers. In the present study, only one farmer used ox for traction; however, the same farmer also used a tractor for farm operations. In spite of low average land holding, the majority of the smallholders in study area hired farm machinery from local villages. This result reflects increasing mechanisation of farming and declining trend of utilisation of animal power in agriculture with respect to the present study area. Ray et al. (1996) and

Valbuena et al. (2011) also reported similar trend of increasing mechanisation and decreasing utility of draft animals in agriculture. It is also important to note that the current attributional approach of LCA would be inadequate to fully account draught function of the smallholder system because it would not tell us about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of fossil fuel required to power farm machinery. Therefore, in such situation, applying consequential approach of LCA would be more appropriate.

Socio-cultural value of bovines was another non-marketable function of the smallholder system. In India, cow rearing is a part of peoples' culture and, many a times, socio-cultural value of bovines is much more important to the farmers than are any other functions. In fact, for all farmers in the study area, bovines were part of their socio-cultural values. We observed that 4 of 60 households in the study area retained six retired animals (two COWS and four buffaloes). In addition to cultural values of bovines, existing anti-slaughter laws in Gujarat could be another reason for retaining unproductive stock by the smallholder farmers. This aspect of smallholder production system in India needs to be explored further before considering socio-cultural value as a function for allocation of GHG emissions. Due to complexity involved in non-marketable functions (draught power and socio- cultural value), they were not considered while allocating GHG emissions in the present study.

Previous study on Kenyan smallholder farms considered milk, meat, finance and insurance under economic allocation and socio- cultural values under livelihood allocation on the basis of farmer perception, by conducting a ranking exercise (Weiler et al. 2014). However, they also reported that only 45% of the farmers in their study area could rank multiple functions of cattle as farmers found ranking exercise outside their scope. In addition, an economic quantification of intangible function such as socio-cultural value was unlikely to meaningfully cover all benefits of cattle rearing to the farmers. Therefore, further research is required for developing methodology for precise quantification of non- marketable functions of bovines and using them for allocating GHG emissions from multi-functional smallholder systems. An economic quantification of marketable and non-marketable functions was applied in dairy production (Bebe et al. 2002); however, whether it covers all contributions of livestock to livelihoods is debatable. For instance, in present study, total economic value of milk includes the value of milk consumed by households plus the surplus milk that was sold for processing. However, an economic-allocation approach in LCA does not quantify nutritional benefits that the milk has provided to the household (Behnke and Muthami 2011).

We found that 30.9-67.8% of the manure was utilised either on- farm or off-farm for its fertiliser value, similar to Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). While computing economic allocation to co- products, economic value of total manure produced on farm (irrespective of its utilisation) was considered. In such circumstances, even if we performed system expansion or used consequential LCA, it would tell us only about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of synthetic fertilisers. In either approach of LCA, the positive impacts that manure has on soil structure, soil organic matter and water holding capacity still remain unexplored. These positive impacts could be an integral part of sustainable nutrient cycle in mixed crop-livestock system (Weiler et al. 2014). Nevertheless, accounting these
positive effects of manure in economic terms is highly complex and challenging, particularly in a multi-functional smallholder production system.
GHG emissions and hotspots

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district was characterised by a mixed crop-livestock system in which an average of 43.0% DM fed to the animals was from crop residues, 18.1% from human-inedible crop by-products, 20.2% from green fodder and 18.7% from processed cattle feed. On average, each farm emitted a total of 10 609.8 kg CO2-eq/year (digestibility allocation basis), of which ~69.3% of emissions were associated with CH4 from enteric fermentation. The higher proportion of CH4 was mostly related to the nature of ruminant digestion and was influenced by the quality of feed (Garg et al. 2013). In our study, an average CH4 emission factor for enteric fermentation was 72.3 and 77.9 kg/year for lactating cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas the feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Therefore, lower digestibility of feed might be associated with higher CH4 emissions due to enteric fermentation (Mangino et al. 2003). The other important aspect influencing total CH4 emissions at farm was the number of non-lactating animals that contributed to the total CH4 emissions. In majority of the smallholder farms, non-lactating animals were mostly the replacement cows and buffaloes, whereas in a few farms, in addition to replacement animals, retired animals and oxen were also present. Although non-lactating animals emitted less CH4 than did lactating animals, from the point of view of production of a single commodity, it is inefficient to keep animals that do not produce milk. However, this is where the importance of valuing multi-functionality begins to have a great effect on CF of milk production. Acknowledging multi-functionality of smallholder farms enabled us to consider non-lactating animals as still being productive, through serving other functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) than producing milk. In the present study, it was difficult to subcategorise young stock into suckling and non- suckling calves, and to obtain exact ages of young stock from farmers was also challenging. Due to the fact that young calves (i.e. less than 1 year of age) produce very limited amounts of CH4 (Singh et al. 2012), we excluded them from the present assessment. Next to enteric CH4, emissions of CO2, CH4 and N20 from feed production together contributed 17.3% to the total GHG emissions of farm. Within farm, GHG emissions from feed production for each category of animal depend on the type of feeds used in the ration of animal. For instance, the higher CO2 emissions for lactating and replacement animals were mainly attributed to feeding of processed feeds (i.e. cattle feed and oilseed meals) and the associated use of fertilisers, pesticides and fossil fuel during feed production. Use of processed feeds for feeding of retired animals was uncommon and this explains the lower feed-related CO2 emissions for these animals. In retired buffaloes, ~51.4% of the feed-related emissions were from CH4, the reason being the use of paddy straw (34-60% in total DM intake) for feeding of these animals. Paddy cultivation is well known for its relatively large CH4 emissions (Phong et al. 2011). For all categories of animals, the emissions of CH4 during feed production were associated with inclusion of crop by-products, i.e. de-oiled rice bran (29.5%) and rice polish (8.0%) in the

concentrate mixture and crop residue (i.e. paddy straw) in the rations. Farmers in the study area managed manure mostly in solid form; therefore, direct and indirect emissions of N20 from manure management had only a small share (<8.1%) in total GHG emissions of the farm. The N20 emissions contributed between 23.0% and 39.8% to the total GHG emissions from feed production and were attributed to application of nitrogenous fertilisers during cultivation. CF of milk production The weighted mean CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production of 60 smallholder farms in Anand was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. In the studied production system, manure had an economic importance for the farmers and, therefore, after applying co-product allocation to manure and other economic functions (finance and insurance), the CF of milk decreased by 22.7% (i.e. 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Similar pattern was also observed for CF of cow and buffalo milk when studied separately. This indicated the role of other economic functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) in sharing environmental burden of milk production in a multi-functional smallholder system. There has been limited research on CF of milk production in smallholder dairy systems. Weiler et al. (2014) reported that CF of milk production in Kenyan smallholder farms was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when only milk and meat were considered as co-products of the system. The CF of milk was further reduced to 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when economic function allocation (including milk, meat, manure, finance and insurance) was applied. Other studies on smallholder dairy systems in Kenya and Ethiopia also reported that CF of milk was changed to 1.5 and 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, respectively, when economic allocation was applied (Modupeore 2011; Woldegebriel 2013). Our findings of CF of milk production are in agreement with Modupeore (2011), Woldegebriel (2013) and Weiler et al. (2014). The CF of energy-corrected milk (ECM) in low productive dairy system of Andean highlands, Peru, was 13.8 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Bartl et al. 2011). The CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) in European countries was 1.6 for The Netherlands (Thomassen et al. 2008b) and 1.1 for Ireland (O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas in Swedish farms, it was 1.1 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Henriksson et al. 2011). The variation in CF of milk production in high- producing dairy systems was reported from 0.8 to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM in Ireland and from 0.8 to 1.3 kg CO2-eq/kg milk in Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) countries (de Vries and de Boer 2010). The annual FPCM yield in European high-producing specialised systems varied from 5380 to 8081 kg/cow (Thomassen et al. 2008b; O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas, in our case, it ranged from 715 to 3925 and from 842 to 4010 kg in cows and buffaloes, respectively. Thus, farms in Anand district produced CF in the range of 0.9-6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. For cow and buffalo milk, cradle-to-farm gate global average emission intensity in mixed system was 2.6 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, whereas in southern Asia, it was 5.5 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, respectively (Gerber et al. 2013). Our findings of CF values of milk production contradict the CF values reported in global studies by FAO (Gerber et al. 2013). Plausible explanations|for this could be the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by FAO. Global studies by FAO have considered N2O emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our study area were stall fed and housed in low-input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk, instead the milk was immediately carried to the village-level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N2O from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in Anand district. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (FAO 2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and Anand district. An estimated average feed digestibility used by FAO (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas, in the present study, feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher CF of milk. Another explanation for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be that an average milk production of cow and buffalo in our study area was 2007 and 1324 kg/year, respectively, whereas FAO (2010) reported an overall annual average of <1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk production levels in global studies used FAO statistical data, which were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014). The relationship between CF and milk output per lactating animal indicted that, with increasing yields, GHG emissions on a lactating-animal basis increased but decreased per kilogram of output (FPCM). Gerber et al. (2011) and Tubiello et al. (2012) also reported a similar relationship between productivity and GHG emissions of dairy production. Gerber et al. (2011) concluded that increasing productivity could be a viable mitigation approach, especially in areas where milk yields are currently below 2000 kg/year.cow. Therefore, in the present study, the possibility for reducing milk CF exists if productivity of animals producing less than 2000 kg milk/year is enhanced. We observed that as the feed digestibility increased, the milk CF decreased per unit of FPCM, with a moderate relationship between them. Lower feed digestibility was mainly attributed to the higher proportion of crop residues (up to 43.0%) in total DM intake of animals. Feed digestibility is among one of the factors influencing enteric CH4 emissions in farm animals (Wilkerson et al. 1994) and low levels (<55%) are often associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions per unit of product (FAO 2000). However, as feed digestibility and production levels increase,

CH4 emissions per unit of product decrease (Boadi et al. 2004). As evident from the present study, enteric CH4 was the major contributor (69.3%) to the total GHG emissions of farms. Therefore, as the feed digestibility increased, total GHG emissions (mainly enteric CH4) decreased, resulting in lower CF per unit of milk.
Mitigation options and implications for policy makers

Results of our study indicated that the CF of milk changes depending on the methodological choice of allocation within LCA. Common mitigation options focus on increased animal productivity through feeding nutritionally balanced rations (Garg et al. 2013) and better manure management (Herrero et al. 2010; Gerber et al. 2011). From the single-commodity point of view, i.e. milk, keeping a lower number of high- producing animals and replacing synthetic fertilisers with manure could result in higher milk production and lower CF of milk. Considering multi-functionality of a smallholder system, reducing the number of animals does not make sense because all animals play important roles and offer economic returns to the farmer. The most applicable mitigation options include improving feed digestibility, enhancing FCE and productivity of animals by feeding nutritionally balancedration (Garg al. 2013), reducing use of synthetic N-based fertilisers, improving efficiency of fertiliser application in crop production (Tirado et al. 2010) and covering the manure heaps with banana leaves to avoid nutrient loss during storage (Gichangi et al. 2007). Another mitigation option that lies outside the system boundary could be the use of biogas as a replacement to dung cakes (Pathak et al. 2009; Sharma and Nema 2013). These mitigation options could be effectively implemented through doorstep extension services to the farmers.

From the point of view of a smallholder farmer, the multiple functions of livestock were the main reasons behind keeping the animals. Contrary to mixed systems, the production of milk is by far the most important reason to keep dairy animals in specialised systems. Due to different nature of both systems, they should not be analysed in a same way, i.e. production units of LCA of specialised systems do not fit to multi-functional mixed systems. The LCA being an industrial tool, when applied to a smallholder dairy system, it poorly grasps the multi-functionality aspect. ISO 14044 (2006) also recommended that comparison among systems should be made on the basis of same functions, quantified by the same functional unit. Thus, a direct comparison of milk CF from dairy systems characterised by different degrees of specialisation is not appropriate if the systems differ in their output. Therefore, in future LCAs on smallholder systems, a serious consideration should be given to multi-functionality aspect. We emphasise that before comparing with smallholder systems, specialised farms would also need to be evaluated considering multi-functionality aspect.

utilisation data when expressed for 60 farms indicated that an average 67.8% of the produced manure was utilised as fertiliser for on-farm crops, 30.9% of the manure was sold as fertiliser (mostly by landless farmers and those who produced surplus manure on farm) and remaining 1.3% was used as fuel. Manure was applied to on-farm crops at an average application rate 70.3 kg N/ha.year. In 8 of 60 farms, an average 10% of the produced manure (290 kg DM/year) was mixed with left-over crop residues to prepare dung cakes, which were then used as replacement to firewood throughout the year. The dung cakes were usually prepared during summer and used as and when required by the households. Besides manure, urea was the most commonly used nitrogenous fertiliser by the farmers. Overall, synthetic fertiliser was applied at 145.7 kg N/ha.year.
GHG emissions of smallholder dairy production

Total GHG emissions and emission hotspots are presented in Table 4. Total GHG emissions after mass, economic and digestibility allocations averaged 11744.2, 9883.1 and 10609.8 kg CO2-eq/year.farm, respectively. On digestibility allocation, the major emission hotspot was methane produced from enteric fermentation (69.3%), followed by GHG emissions produced during feed production (17.3%). GHG emissions during feed production occurred due to cultivation, production, processing and transportation of off-farm and on-farm feeds. Emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O from cattle contributed 11.0%, 75.4% and 13.6%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions. The contribution of CO2, CH4 and N2O from buffalo was 8.2%, 80.5% and 11.3%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions of farms. Thus, enteric CH4 was the highest contributing GHG in total farm emissions.

Within herd, emission hotspots for different categories of animals based on digestibility allocation are presented in Fig. 3. In all categories of animals, CH4 emission from enteric fermentation (66.8-78.7%) and CO2, CH4 and N2O emissions from feed production (8.2-19.7%) were the two major hotspots. GHG emissions from manure management contributed least (less than 8.1%) to the total emissions from each category of animal. Retired (old unproductive) cows and buffalos emitted
an average of 1571.3 and 2556.1 kg CO2-eq/retirement year, respectively. GHG emissions from oxen were 3022.1 CO2-eq/ year.

The combined GHG emissions from off-farm and on-farm feed production (Fig. indicated that CO2 was the major hotspot (41.4-55.1%) in total feed-related GHG emissions for lactating and replacement animals. For retired animals, comparatively lower CO2 emissions were recorded than for any other category of animals. In case of retired buffaloes, CH4 was the highest contributing GHG (51.4%) to the total emissions from feed production. The contributions of CO2, CH4 and N2O to the total GHG emissions for oxen were 35.3%, 24.9% and 39.8%, respectively.
CF of milk production using different allocation methods

The CF of milk production under smallholder production system is presented in Table 5. The results of CF of milk production differed depending on the allocation method applied. When all the GHG emissions were allocated to a single product, i.e. 100% allocation to milk, the CF of cow milk was 2.3, 1.9 and 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM on mass, economic and digestibility basis, respectively; whereas, for buffalo milk, the CF was 3.0, 2.5 and 2.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM, respectively. When the milk, manure, finance and insurance were considered as economic functions of the smallholder system, average 78.8% of emissions were allocated to milk and remaining were allocated to other functions (i.e. 4.2% to manure, 12.5% to finance and 4.5% to insurance). Thus, the CF of milk based on economic allocation to co-products or functions was 1.6 and 1.9 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM in COWS and buffaloes, respectively. Figure 5 indicates across the 60 dairy farms that there was significant variability in CF of milk among farms, ranging from 0.9 to 6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. Overall, the mean CF of milk to the farm-gate, weighted by farm milk production was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, which reduced to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM when economic allocation to co-products or functions was applied.

Relationship between CF and feed digestibility

The relationship between milk CF and feed digestibility is presented in Fig. 7. It was observed that with increasing digestibility of rations, the GHG emissions decreased per unit of FPCM, indicating a moderate relationship (R2 = 0.30) between
feed digestibility and CF of milk. For farms in the study area, an average feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%.
Discussion
Smallholder farming system

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district represents a mixed crop-livestock system, in which farmers rely on crop by- products and residues for livestock feeding. The average age of livestock owners in the present study area was in line with Ravikumar et al. (2007) and Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). The average farm land owned by smallholders in Gujarat state was reported between 0.7 and 1.6 ha/household (Staal et al. 2006), which agreed with our findings on land holdings. Information on

herd size, bodyweight, AFC and milk production performance were in agreement with those in earlier reports (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Pundir 2002; Singh and Barwal 2010). A wide range of stocking rate (1-51 adult cattle unit/ha) was reported in semiarid regions of India (Shankar and Gupta 1992), which agreed with our findings on stocking rate in the study area. Similar to present findings, ~10-39% of the cow dung was reported to be utilised as fuel (Valbuena et al. 2011), and varying proportions of dung as manure in Indian villages (Thirunavukkarasu et al. 2014). The farmers in Anand district used less fossil fuel for on-farm crop production than did those in European farms (O'Brien et al. 2014; 97.0 VS 14.8 L/ha).

Smallholders in tropical regions of the world rear bovines for multiple functions (Moll et al. 2007; Weiler et al. 2014); therefore, we emphasise that all functions should be considered while handling environmental burden in smallholder production system. In such system, the various ways that livestock serve their owners could be stated as products or in LCA terms, co- products, which are one or more products coming from same the production system (ISO 14044 2006). Multiple functions of livestock could be grouped as marketable (i.e. those having direct or indirect economic value) and non-marketable functions (i.e. those that are difficult to quantify in economic terms). Marketable functions with direct economic value include milk and manure, and provide regular economic returns to farmers. For example, smallholder farmers in Anand district earned daily economic returns from sale of milk and quarterly economic returns from sale of manure. Functions with indirect market value (finance and insurance) provide seldom economic returns to farmers; nevertheless, their importance for smallholder households with limited financial ability cannot be ignored. Farmers in our study area were ready to sell their animals in situations of urgent cash requirement, such as, for example, meeting medical expenses, purchasing land and function like marriage. Bebe et al. (2002) and Weiler et al. (2014) also reported similar situations in which Kenyan smallholder farmers exchanged their animals to cash.

Non-marketable functions include role of cattle as drought power and socio-cultural values. We observed that the use of cattle as draught power was an individual preference of the farmers. In the present study, only one farmer used ox for traction; however, the same farmer also used a tractor for farm operations. In spite of low average land holding, the majority of the smallholders in study area hired farm machinery from local villages. This result reflects increasing mechanisation of farming and declining trend of utilisation of animal power in agriculture with respect to the present study area. Ray et al. (1996) and

Valbuena et al. (2011) also reported similar trend of increasing mechanisation and decreasing utility of draft animals in agriculture. It is also important to note that the current attributional approach of LCA would be inadequate to fully account draught function of the smallholder system because it would not tell us about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of fossil fuel required to power farm machinery. Therefore, in such situation, applying consequential approach of LCA would be more appropriate.

Socio-cultural value of bovines was another non-marketable function of the smallholder system. In India, cow rearing is a part of peoples' culture and, many a times, socio-cultural value of bovines is much more important to the farmers than are any other functions. In fact, for all farmers in the study area, bovines were part of their socio-cultural values. We observed that 4 of 60 households in the study area retained six retired animals (two COWS and four buffaloes). In addition to cultural values of bovines, existing anti-slaughter laws in Gujarat could be another reason for retaining unproductive stock by the smallholder farmers. This aspect of smallholder production system in India needs to be explored further before considering socio-cultural value as a function for allocation of GHG emissions. Due to complexity involved in non-marketable functions (draught power and socio- cultural value), they were not considered while allocating GHG emissions in the present study.

Previous study on Kenyan smallholder farms considered milk, meat, finance and insurance under economic allocation and socio- cultural values under livelihood allocation on the basis of farmer perception, by conducting a ranking exercise (Weiler et al. 2014). However, they also reported that only 45% of the farmers in their study area could rank multiple functions of cattle as farmers found ranking exercise outside their scope. In addition, an economic quantification of intangible function such as socio-cultural value was unlikely to meaningfully cover all benefits of cattle rearing to the farmers. Therefore, further research is required for developing methodology for precise quantification of non- marketable functions of bovines and using them for allocating GHG emissions from multi-functional smallholder systems. An economic quantification of marketable and non-marketable functions was applied in dairy production (Bebe et al. 2002); however, whether it covers all contributions of livestock to livelihoods is debatable. For instance, in present study, total economic value of milk includes the value of milk consumed by households plus the surplus milk that was sold for processing. However, an economic-allocation approach in LCA does not quantify nutritional benefits that the milk has provided to the household (Behnke and Muthami 2011).

We found that 30.9-67.8% of the manure was utilised either on- farm or off-farm for its fertiliser value, similar to Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). While computing economic allocation to co- products, economic value of total manure produced on farm (irrespective of its utilisation) was considered. In such circumstances, even if we performed system expansion or used consequential LCA, it would tell us only about the net change in GHG emissions due to replacement of synthetic fertilisers. In either approach of LCA, the positive impacts that manure has on soil structure, soil organic matter and water holding capacity still remain unexplored. These positive impacts could be an integral part of sustainable nutrient cycle in mixed crop-livestock system (Weiler et al. 2014). Nevertheless, accounting these
positive effects of manure in economic terms is highly complex and challenging, particularly in a multi-functional smallholder production system.
GHG emissions and hotspots

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district was characterised by a mixed crop-livestock system in which an average of 43.0% DM fed to the animals was from crop residues, 18.1% from human-inedible crop by-products, 20.2% from green fodder and 18.7% from processed cattle feed. On average, each farm emitted a total of 10 609.8 kg CO2-eq/year (digestibility allocation basis), of which ~69.3% of emissions were associated with CH4 from enteric fermentation. The higher proportion of CH4 was mostly related to the nature of ruminant digestion and was influenced by the quality of feed (Garg et al. 2013). In our study, an average CH4 emission factor for enteric fermentation was 72.3 and 77.9 kg/year for lactating cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas the feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Therefore, lower digestibility of feed might be associated with higher CH4 emissions due to enteric fermentation (Mangino et al. 2003). The other important aspect influencing total CH4 emissions at farm was the number of non-lactating animals that contributed to the total CH4 emissions. In majority of the smallholder farms, non-lactating animals were mostly the replacement cows and buffaloes, whereas in a few farms, in addition to replacement animals, retired animals and oxen were also present. Although non-lactating animals emitted less CH4 than did lactating animals, from the point of view of production of a single commodity, it is inefficient to keep animals that do not produce milk. However, this is where the importance of valuing multi-functionality begins to have a great effect on CF of milk production. Acknowledging multi-functionality of smallholder farms enabled us to consider non-lactating animals as still being productive, through serving other functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) than producing milk. In the present study, it was difficult to subcategorise young stock into suckling and non- suckling calves, and to obtain exact ages of young stock from farmers was also challenging. Due to the fact that young calves (i.e. less than 1 year of age) produce very limited amounts of CH4 (Singh et al. 2012), we excluded them from the present assessment. Next to enteric CH4, emissions of CO2, CH4 and N20 from feed production together contributed 17.3% to the total GHG emissions of farm. Within farm, GHG emissions from feed production for each category of animal depend on the type of feeds used in the ration of animal. For instance, the higher CO2 emissions for lactating and replacement animals were mainly attributed to feeding of processed feeds (i.e. cattle feed and oilseed meals) and the associated use of fertilisers, pesticides and fossil fuel during feed production. Use of processed feeds for feeding of retired animals was uncommon and this explains the lower feed-related CO2 emissions for these animals. In retired buffaloes, ~51.4% of the feed-related emissions were from CH4, the reason being the use of paddy straw (34-60% in total DM intake) for feeding of these animals. Paddy cultivation is well known for its relatively large CH4 emissions (Phong et al. 2011). For all categories of animals, the emissions of CH4 during feed production were associated with inclusion of crop by-products, i.e. de-oiled rice bran (29.5%) and rice polish (8.0%) in the

concentrate mixture and crop residue (i.e. paddy straw) in the rations. Farmers in the study area managed manure mostly in solid form; therefore, direct and indirect emissions of N20 from manure management had only a small share (<8.1%) in total GHG emissions of the farm. The N20 emissions contributed between 23.0% and 39.8% to the total GHG emissions from feed production and were attributed to application of nitrogenous fertilisers during cultivation. CF of milk production The weighted mean CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production of 60 smallholder farms in Anand was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. In the studied production system, manure had an economic importance for the farmers and, therefore, after applying co-product allocation to manure and other economic functions (finance and insurance), the CF of milk decreased by 22.7% (i.e. 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Similar pattern was also observed for CF of cow and buffalo milk when studied separately. This indicated the role of other economic functions (i.e. manure, finance and insurance) in sharing environmental burden of milk production in a multi-functional smallholder system. There has been limited research on CF of milk production in smallholder dairy systems. Weiler et al. (2014) reported that CF of milk production in Kenyan smallholder farms was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when only milk and meat were considered as co-products of the system. The CF of milk was further reduced to 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk when economic function allocation (including milk, meat, manure, finance and insurance) was applied. Other studies on smallholder dairy systems in Kenya and Ethiopia also reported that CF of milk was changed to 1.5 and 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, respectively, when economic allocation was applied (Modupeore 2011; Woldegebriel 2013). Our findings of CF of milk production are in agreement with Modupeore (2011), Woldegebriel (2013) and Weiler et al. (2014). The CF of energy-corrected milk (ECM) in low productive dairy system of Andean highlands, Peru, was 13.8 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Bartl et al. 2011). The CF of cradle-to-farm gate milk production (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) in European countries was 1.6 for The Netherlands (Thomassen et al. 2008b) and 1.1 for Ireland (O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas in Swedish farms, it was 1.1 kg CO2-eq/kg ECM (Henriksson et al. 2011). The variation in CF of milk production in high- producing dairy systems was reported from 0.8 to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM in Ireland and from 0.8 to 1.3 kg CO2-eq/kg milk in Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) countries (de Vries and de Boer 2010). The annual FPCM yield in European high-producing specialised systems varied from 5380 to 8081 kg/cow (Thomassen et al. 2008b; O'Brien et al. 2014), whereas, in our case, it ranged from 715 to 3925 and from 842 to 4010 kg in cows and buffaloes, respectively. Thus, farms in Anand district produced CF in the range of 0.9-6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. For cow and buffalo milk, cradle-to-farm gate global average emission intensity in mixed system was 2.6 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, whereas in southern Asia, it was 5.5 and 3.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, respectively (Gerber et al. 2013). Our findings of CF values of milk production contradict the CF values reported in global studies by FAO (Gerber et al. 2013). Plausible explanations|for this could be the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by FAO. Global studies by FAO have considered N2O emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our study area were stall fed and housed in low-input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk, instead the milk was immediately carried to the village-level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N2O from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in Anand district. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (FAO 2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and Anand district. An estimated average feed digestibility used by FAO (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo, respectively, whereas, in the present study, feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%. Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher CF of milk. Another explanation for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be that an average milk production of cow and buffalo in our study area was 2007 and 1324 kg/year, respectively, whereas FAO (2010) reported an overall annual average of <1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk production levels in global studies used FAO statistical data, which were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014). The relationship between CF and milk output per lactating animal indicted that, with increasing yields, GHG emissions on a lactating-animal basis increased but decreased per kilogram of output (FPCM). Gerber et al. (2011) and Tubiello et al. (2012) also reported a similar relationship between productivity and GHG emissions of dairy production. Gerber et al. (2011) concluded that increasing productivity could be a viable mitigation approach, especially in areas where milk yields are currently below 2000 kg/year.cow. Therefore, in the present study, the possibility for reducing milk CF exists if productivity of animals producing less than 2000 kg milk/year is enhanced. We observed that as the feed digestibility increased, the milk CF decreased per unit of FPCM, with a moderate relationship between them. Lower feed digestibility was mainly attributed to the higher proportion of crop residues (up to 43.0%) in total DM intake of animals. Feed digestibility is among one of the factors influencing enteric CH4 emissions in farm animals (Wilkerson et al. 1994) and low levels (<55%) are often associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions per unit of product (FAO 2000). However, as feed digestibility and production levels increase,

CH4 emissions per unit of product decrease (Boadi et al. 2004). As evident from the present study, enteric CH4 was the major contributor (69.3%) to the total GHG emissions of farms. Therefore, as the feed digestibility increased, total GHG emissions (mainly enteric CH4) decreased, resulting in lower CF per unit of milk.
Mitigation options and implications for policy makers

Results of our study indicated that the CF of milk changes depending on the methodological choice of allocation within LCA. Common mitigation options focus on increased animal productivity through feeding nutritionally balanced rations (Garg et al. 2013) and better manure management (Herrero et al. 2010; Gerber et al. 2011). From the single-commodity point of view, i.e. milk, keeping a lower number of high- producing animals and replacing synthetic fertilisers with manure could result in higher milk production and lower CF of milk. Considering multi-functionality of a smallholder system, reducing the number of animals does not make sense because all animals play important roles and offer economic returns to the farmer. The most applicable mitigation options include improving feed digestibility, enhancing FCE and productivity of animals by feeding nutritionally balancedration (Garg al. 2013), reducing use of synthetic N-based fertilisers, improving efficiency of fertiliser application in crop production (Tirado et al. 2010) and covering the manure heaps with banana leaves to avoid nutrient loss during storage (Gichangi et al. 2007). Another mitigation option that lies outside the system boundary could be the use of biogas as a replacement to dung cakes (Pathak et al. 2009; Sharma and Nema 2013). These mitigation options could be effectively implemented through doorstep extension services to the farmers.

From the point of view of a smallholder farmer, the multiple functions of livestock were the main reasons behind keeping the animals. Contrary to mixed systems, the production of milk is by far the most important reason to keep dairy animals in specialised systems. Due to different nature of both systems, they should not be analysed in a same way, i.e. production units of LCA of specialised systems do not fit to multi-functional mixed systems. The LCA being an industrial tool, when applied to a smallholder dairy system, it poorly grasps the multi-functionality aspect. ISO 14044 (2006) also recommended that comparison among systems should be made on the basis of same functions, quantified by the same functional unit. Thus, a direct comparison of milk CF from dairy systems characterised by different degrees of specialisation is not appropriate if the systems differ in their output. Therefore, in future LCAs on smallholder systems, a serious consideration should be given to multi-functionality aspect. We emphasise that before comparing with smallholder systems, specialised farms would also need to be evaluated considering multi-functionality aspect.

utilisation data when expressed for 60 farms indicated that an average 67.8% of the produced manure was utilised as fertiliser for on-farm crops, 30.9% of the manure was sold as fertiliser (mostly by landless farmers and those who produced surplus manure on farm) and remaining 1.3% was used as fuel. Manure was applied to on-farm crops at an average application rate 70.3 kg N/ha.year. In 8 of 60 farms, an average 10% of the produced manure (290 kg DM/year) was mixed with left-over crop residues to prepare dung cakes, which were then used as replacement to firewood throughout the year. The dung cakes were usually prepared during summer and used as and when required by the households. Besides manure, urea was the most commonly used nitrogenous fertiliser by the farmers. Overall, synthetic fertiliser was applied at 145.7 kg N/ha.year.
GHG emissions of smallholder dairy production

Total GHG emissions and emission hotspots are presented in Table 4. Total GHG emissions after mass, economic and digestibility allocations averaged 11744.2, 9883.1 and 10609.8 kg CO2-eq/year.farm, respectively. On digestibility allocation, the major emission hotspot was methane produced from enteric fermentation (69.3%), followed by GHG emissions produced during feed production (17.3%). GHG emissions during feed production occurred due to cultivation, production, processing and transportation of off-farm and on-farm feeds. Emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O from cattle contributed 11.0%, 75.4% and 13.6%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions. The contribution of CO2, CH4 and N2O from buffalo was 8.2%, 80.5% and 11.3%, respectively, to the total GHG emissions of farms. Thus, enteric CH4 was the highest contributing GHG in total farm emissions.

Within herd, emission hotspots for different categories of animals based on digestibility allocation are presented in Fig. 3. In all categories of animals, CH4 emission from enteric fermentation (66.8-78.7%) and CO2, CH4 and N2O emissions from feed production (8.2-19.7%) were the two major hotspots. GHG emissions from manure management contributed least (less than 8.1%) to the total emissions from each category of animal. Retired (old unproductive) cows and buffalos emitted
an average of 1571.3 and 2556.1 kg CO2-eq/retirement year, respectively. GHG emissions from oxen were 3022.1 CO2-eq/ year.

The combined GHG emissions from off-farm and on-farm feed production (Fig. indicated that CO2 was the major hotspot (41.4-55.1%) in total feed-related GHG emissions for lactating and replacement animals. For retired animals, comparatively lower CO2 emissions were recorded than for any other category of animals. In case of retired buffaloes, CH4 was the highest contributing GHG (51.4%) to the total emissions from feed production. The contributions of CO2, CH4 and N2O to the total GHG emissions for oxen were 35.3%, 24.9% and 39.8%, respectively.
CF of milk production using different allocation methods

The CF of milk production under smallholder production system is presented in Table 5. The results of CF of milk production differed depending on the allocation method applied. When all the GHG emissions were allocated to a single product, i.e. 100% allocation to milk, the CF of cow milk was 2.3, 1.9 and 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM on mass, economic and digestibility basis, respectively; whereas, for buffalo milk, the CF was 3.0, 2.5 and 2.7 kg CO2-eq/ kg FPCM, respectively. When the milk, manure, finance and insurance were considered as economic functions of the smallholder system, average 78.8% of emissions were allocated to milk and remaining were allocated to other functions (i.e. 4.2% to manure, 12.5% to finance and 4.5% to insurance). Thus, the CF of milk based on economic allocation to co-products or functions was 1.6 and 1.9 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM in COWS and buffaloes, respectively. Figure 5 indicates across the 60 dairy farms that there was significant variability in CF of milk among farms, ranging from 0.9 to 6.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM. Overall, the mean CF of milk to the farm-gate, weighted by farm milk production was 2.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, which reduced to 1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM when economic allocation to co-products or functions was applied.

Relationship between CF and feed digestibility

The relationship between milk CF and feed digestibility is presented in Fig. 7. It was observed that with increasing digestibility of rations, the GHG emissions decreased per unit of FPCM, indicating a moderate relationship (R2 = 0.30) between
feed digestibility and CF of milk. For farms in the study area, an average feed digestibility was estimated at 59.2%.
Discussion
Smallholder farming system

The smallholder dairy farming in Anand district represents a mixed crop-livestock system, in which farmers rely on crop by- products and residues for livestock feeding. The average age of livestock owners in the present study area was in line with Ravikumar et al. (2007) and Thirunavukkarasu et al. (2014). The average farm land owned by smallholders in Gujarat state was reported between 0.7 and 1.6 ha/household (Staal et al. 2006), which agreed with our findings on land holdings. Information on
